          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Chapter 1\n\nRain slicked the Narrowvale post. Jhace climbed, glove sticking on wet rung, shoulder ache flaring and ignored. He set the backboard to the iron straps and drove the first screw until the plate rang level. Air pressed from the choke like a held breath finding a seam.\n\n“Cordon two,” he said down into the noise.\n\nBrighlee clipped the caller strip high and clean, the small symbol bright against fog. She didn’t decorate it with talk. Kylar walked the first three carts with two fingers and a stare, turning axles, making space without touch. A mule rattled harness. Steam lifted. The lane started to remember how to flow.\n\nJhace chalked the map onto the board in four lines, not a drawing, a route. Map-as-Set. He marked the exits, the cut-in, the lift that always snagged. No sermon about why it worked. He set it to work. “Caller window here,” he said, tapping the square. Brighlee flipped the cloth to Yellow and tied it loose so a knife could pull it fast.\n\nHe checked the ridger. Teeth clean. A clause plate waited in his pocket, edges blunted so it would teach without cutting. He took it out and pressed its lip to the top left, then felt for the bite. Good. He tamped once with the heel of his hand and the ridge took its seat.\n\nTwo short taps on the post with the coin cap of his rod. The sound carried. Someone in the crush answered by shifting half a step. Then someone else. Kylar’s hands showed a cart where to live. The lane’s pressure eased from shove to glide.\n\nHe kept moving. Chalk. Tap. Set. He cut a notch for the corner pinch two carts down and posted a second small plate beside the hazard icon: no words, just the wedge glyph pointing the right way. The board wasn’t a prayer. It was a set of actions people could make in time.\n\nHoarseness scratched at his throat. He didn’t drink. He steadied his hand and placed the Conclave clause where it would be seen without being stared at, lower right. He seated the Tempermark die and put weight behind it. Ring, bite, hold. He lifted the stamp and checked the ridge with the side of his thumb. Honest.\n\nA call flared near the tea stalls and tried to start trouble in the wrong lane. Jhace didn’t look over. Two taps again, same spacing, and Kylar slid his palm along a cart’s shaft like he was balancing air; the trouble bent along the posted line and became nothing. A wheel creaked, then found quiet.\n\n“Board reads,” Jhace said.\n\nBrighlee had the pen already writing. Functional. She posted the small slip with the clause number and stepped back. The crowd, without quite thinking, matched her distance.\n\nJhace knelt on the midstep and set a small wedge into the pavement, low and flat, where barrows always drifted. One tap. The wedge talked to the wheel before the wheel could pretend not to hear. He checked the notch with his thumb. Set.\n\nThe choke widened in the way a throat widens when pain loosens. He listened for ring under stone. Even. No chatter. The ear ring from earlier faded to a dull, acceptable hum.\n\nCosts: glove sticks; hoarse caller. He didn’t name them out loud. He kept the work moving.\n\nA man in red sleeves tried to invent a lane. Kylar pointed once, and the man’s idea fell apart without needing its pride killed. Brighlee flipped the caller back to Blue, ticked a half-knot into the tie, and clipped a single narrow slip under the larger clause: window closed at Bell. Posted.\n\nJhace wiped rain from the clause plate with the back of his wrist and gave it one more seat. He checked the ridge again, thumb across. He didn’t love the ink’s shine. He wanted the matte that meant it would read in bad light. He took a breath, set the stamp again beside the first mark, not required, but present for people who count. Bite. Done.\n\nHe held still long enough to let the lane tell him if it had learned. Pressure at the choke went from thrust to glide. He stepped back.\n\n“Done,” he told Brighlee.\n\nShe closed her book without flourish.\n\nKylar drifted back in with a spare rope coil, not needed anymore. He stood at the edge of the board and read it once like a person memorizing a route he would need later in a different city.\n\nJhace tipped his chin toward the square. “There. The clause is that this is the clause.” He didn’t wait for agreement. He rubbed the plate’s lower corner with his thumb to see if the ridge held under rain and flesh. It did.\n\nBrighlee unrolled a fresh caller strip and slipped it over the post so the next crew would know where to stand and when. Kylar moved through the first carts like he had been born a knot-opener, touching nothing, only pointing. A mule snorted. A wheel creaked. Steam lifted. The corridor’s pitch lowered.\n\nJhace stepped away, hoarseness a scratch that wouldn’t drink water until the work was done. He let the lane show him it had learned. It had. The choke’s push faded to glide.\n\nHe set the stamp one last time, small bite beside the first, then ran his own thumb along the Tempermark’s ridge where the legend lived. Skin found raised line by instinct, not sight.\n\nA woman with a market cart did the same, tentative, then sure. Her thumb found the ridge. The cart eased forward without a shouted order.\n\n# Chapter 2\n\nTiffani lowered the lamps until the glass told the truth and chalked the wedge glyph on the board angled to the corner work. The metronome bell ticked two beats soft. “Wedge before veil,” she said, voice clean the way you speak beside reactive material. “Corners, three-count. Dead panes first.” She hooked the first pane and traced its route with a raw wedge plate, letting the edge print the turn into her wrist.\n\nHot racks arrived off schedule, bars still giving heat. Steam lifted when rain off coats met metal. Salee took the oil shelf with a wrist tap that said touch nothing you can’t put back. Kylar stood quiet at the edge with ties he didn’t need. Valaryn laid return-to-work slips where eyes could read and hands could fail to touch.\n\n“The wedge points; the tool speaks; they must agree,” Tiffani said. She seated a corner and pressed until the pane stopped lying. The bell ticked twice. The room learned to hold its breath and then to let it go.\n\nA porter at the clinic door fumbled the brace. The sill had a proud point that had taught itself to rise under years of wet and yesterday’s surge. Tiffani knelt to sight along timber and stone. “Left brace a thumb higher. Right, feather. Don’t drag.” Tap tap. The porter’s wrapped hand shook, then steadied when his palm found the ring again. The door moved through its last third; the creak dropped from raw to shallow scrape. Salee touched oil to the pin and wiped the extra so grit wouldn’t take the gift back later.\n\nInside, a trainee tried to help by pulling. Helping is sabotage with manners. Tiffani raised a palm in the hinge gap. “Outside binds. Inside waits.” The girl stitched her hands into her belt. They held the door half-open for a count; heat went out, iron cooled a degree. Tap tap. Through the full motion now. The timber let go a long, thrummed sigh and came to seat in silence.\n\nJhace appeared at the arch’s edge, water sliding from his hem to the stone where it belonged. He set one hand on the home ring and steadied the brace with the other without talk. Their eyes met for a beat. The spark she had shelved put a warm bead in her throat and slid lower. She kept it; she kept working.\n\n“Card,” she said. Salee had the kit open. The old door drill card on the inner wall showed wedge small, ring at waist height, three bars for count, oil droplet, clinic ⭕ in the corner. It lacked the bind clause the room had just learned with its shoulders.\n\nTiffani pulled a blank, inked the wedge larger, and moved it upstream of the ring to teach set corner first even at a hinge. Under it she laid a simple bind: a bar crossing a ring, two small hands as half-circles. **Micro-Bind: one unit, caller pairs** lived in icon plates, not text. She stamped the clinic ⭕ lower right and the small Conclave quill square bottom left. She pressed the ridger across the legend so the board would tell on itself if anyone tried to lift it. Thumb across: bite.\n\nJall Var’s mouth shaped a flourish request near the lower margin. She fed it nothing. “East gate,” she said, and walked there because the wedge angle had been trying to lie. She corrected it. The room felt the correction like a knuckle cracked clean; the next cart took the turn without shriek. Sound fell from the lane like a dropped habit.\n\n“Board plates match,” Chloen said, setting three overlays on the shelf in a row. Arrow tooth here, chalk there. “West, Center, East: the same story.” Tiffani stood back until all three fit in her head, eyes blur, then sharp. The maps spoke one language. People began to behave like citizens of a single corridor instead of ambassadors from their own weather. She let herself feel the cost finally: grit at the corners of her eyes, not grit at all, only muscle asking to rest. She put a thumb to Center’s ridged patch and took its small stubbornness back into her hand.\n\n“Done,” she told the boards.\n\nBrighlee tied a small caller note under each map, icon for **one hand on home** beside a bell mark for night crews. Kylar rubbed the ring glyph under West with his whole palm and grinned at no one when the paint shone more honest.\n\nTiffani pressed the quill square on East one last time and tied the lower left corner again because corners are where lies start. The bell down-lane gave its seam. Crews paused and then chose lanes the maps had already given them. Pitch lowered. Steam drew polite ribbons. The clerk trio that had tried to be important wandered off to inspect somewhere else.\n\nShe slid the kit under her coat. Two fingertips to Center’s ridged patch. Those fingertips to the temple. The world sharpened a fraction. She breathed once and let the corridor answer if it had learned.\n\nOn the wall, three Map-as-Set boards hung at West, Center, and East, identical: arrows meeting at a tooth, ring at waist height, wedge set honest, bell pairs along the bottom, quill squares biting dark. At each caller post, a small square sat at the same height, marked and ready. In the slick light, the three maps read like one drafted sentence, and a market cart’s thumb found the ridge and moved.\n\n# Chapter 3\n\nRain turned Narrowvale’s cobbles to iron plates and pushed steam low along the shins. The choke was already fraying: carts angling for advantage, runners trying to split the same lane, voices building pressure for no reason except habit. Jhace felt it all through the caller post, palm flat to the old wood. The grain carried the lane’s trouble back into his bones, granular, no flow.\n\nHe lifted the short bar. Tempered rod. Coin-thick cap. Work, not theater.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nEdges found ears. The second proved the first wasn’t stray. He breathed to keep the spacing honest and did it again. Two short. The weather needled his sleeve and the cap gave back a clean ring off the stone face. Brighlee flipped the caller square from Blue to Yellow and tied fast for quick cut if the wind asked. Kylar drifted the left boundary, patient hand on a red-sleeved vendor’s axle, nudging him back to the heavy lane like it was always his idea.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nA shove rose near the steam carts and shook the middle like a wire. Jhace didn’t look. He counted heads downstream: four, then six, moving in the cadence. No orders. Just timing. The board already told them what. He gave them when. He kept the bar in his left to save the right for fine work.\n\nThe first knot loosened where runners had tried to share space with a barrow. He shifted the beat one rib wider and let the sound bounce and return corrected. The lane’s breath caught, then evened. “Yellow holds,” Brighlee said, voice flat and functional from the post. Her pencil worked in the wet, steady.\n\nThe bell rope twitched down the corridor. Window shift coming. He set the bar under his arm and shouldered to the center seam, a step without apology. Two short taps. Pause. Two. The pause mattered. Crowds hate blank. He gave them a thin one that would hold.\n\nA girl with oil tins misjudged a shoulder. She swallowed the pain and kept her feet. He kept his eyes on distant heads. Reaction travels. He would rather shape it than chase it.\n\nKylar’s cord line kissed the ground in quick loops. He didn’t try to make a fence out of string. He made suggestions. Runners read suggestions faster than speech. Red Sleeves tried to angle again and met the cord lightly on his shin. He looked for a human to quarrel with and found none. He settled.\n\nRain thickened. The lane answered.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nAt the far curb a boy popped a kettle lid for steam and took the corner of the world with it. Fog rolled. Sound pressed flatter. Jhace felt his rib tempo get tempted and denied it. He set his boot against the post and let the ring run through tendon rather than skull. Ear ring rose like a weather front and sat there, a cost he’d pay later. Not now.\n\nA cart wheel slid on iron wet. He changed nothing. Panic rewards itself. The driver corrected to the cadence and only then heard his own breath. Jhace shifted the bar to mark a side-pulse for foot traffic. Two short, then a half-beat ghost for walkers. Heads turned like birds. The wire-hum of argument at the edge fell a measure.\n\nHe palmed his glove to wipe rain. The leather stuck to his knuckles. Glove sticks, noted. He didn’t look at the ache blooming in the shoulder where yesterday’s brace had asked more than a day’s worth. Shoulder ache, noted. The work paid attention to those who didn’t ask it to pretend.\n\nYellow bought him half a Bell. Yellow was all he wanted.\n\n“Clause?” Brighlee asked without lifting her eyes. The pencil did its own work.\n\n“Ridge the caller, not the crowd,” he said. She didn’t need the why. He wasn’t sermonizing. He was naming parts. She set her ridger teeth through waxed strip and wrote what would be posted if it held.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nA row of kettles chose to squeal at the same note. Tuning didn’t matter. What mattered was borders. He gave the edge back to them with the bar. She wrote: Pressline cadence in effect. Two-short marks. Obey Yellow. Brace taps only in Bells Two and Three. If it isn’t on the board, it didn’t happen. She underlined the Tempermark space and left it blank. He had to earn ink.\n\nA second knot built at the mouth where the street narrowed around a bolted drain. He tracked the cause: a boy avoiding an ankle-high puddle, forcing a woman to step into the heavy lane, forcing a cart to lose angle, forcing four shoulders to close. All of it small and enough. He changed the measure to two short, long breath, two. The breath gave the puddle room to be water instead of law. The knot unwound with the laziness of a salvaged thread.\n\nHe set his palm to the post again. Tone came back even. The lane had stopped insulting itself. He took a shorter stance. Test whether it was him or the board. He halved the volume, then halved it again. The lane carried itself. That’s what you wanted.\n\nA body crashed into his shoulder. Red Sleeves. He’d found a human after all and didn’t like the absence of quarrel. Jhace didn’t step back. The bar remained a tool, not a weapon. He didn’t look at the eyes. He looked at the axle. Two short taps, and his ribs stayed ruler-straight. Red Sleeves’ anger slid off the edges of timing. The man retreated because there was nowhere for his noise to live.\n\nKylar’s coil shortened. He’d learned something today. Good.\n\nBrighlee lifted a fresh strip from the kit and added one line beneath the clause: Caller square changes set cadence. Watch the cloth. She kept it functional, not clever. When she clipped it for a test fit to the lower board, the ink bled but held.\n\nBell Four’s face came up. The rope rang, light and fast along the corridor. A shiver went through the whole shape of the choke. Jhace narrowed the spacing and moved two paces toward the bridge side without breaking count. He did not fight the bell. He escorted it. Two short taps. The sound laid a rail for the new flow to choose. It chose.\n\nA runner skated, caught himself on a post, swore until he saw the Yellow and read his own mouth shut. The square isn’t a suggestion. It’s a contract.\n\nThe ear ring finally took its tax. A low thrumming behind his right eye made the bar’s cap look brighter than it should. He swapped hands for a few beats to spare the burn he’d hidden with wrap. Blister bite woke under the glove. He could admire pain later. Now he counted and listened.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nThe choke thinned from crush to queue. The fog lifted to knee height. The puddle at the drain stopped bossing the lane. Red Sleeves became red sleeves again.\n\nBrighlee stood with her pencil paused. “Status?” she said, five letters of mercy. He put the barhead to the post and gave three quick, quiet kisses of brass to wood, the board’s home code for Steady under new face. She wrote it, plain.\n\nKylar let the cord lie slack and then reeled it in, loops flat, not tangled. He’d make a decent clerk someday if the world didn’t eat him first.\n\nJhace stopped tapping.\n\nThe lane kept moving.\n\nHe gave it ten counts to prove it would live without him. It did. He leaned the bar against the post and flexed the web of his hand. The glove’s seam had raised a ridge. He pressed it flat with his thumb until the sting settled into something he would remember later.\n\n“Post it,” he said.\n\nBrighlee pulled the clause sheet and slid it into the glass sleeve on the caller board. She wiped the pane with the heel of her hand and stamped the Tempermark into the wax with a single, level bite. Ink haloed and went still. Kylar tied off the Yellow square at half-staff for the next team’s eyes.\n\nJhace walked the first twenty paces downstream, not as a guard, only to test. Foot traffic matched the two-short in their own heads now, shoulders falling into the same slow angle of mercy people rediscover when the lane reminds them it’s shared. He checked the tricky drain one more time. The water had taken a longer path. Good.\n\nHe came back to the post and let his shoulders drop, a weight setting into the bracket cut for it. Hoarse caller burned the top of his throat. He drank from the bottle and tasted glass.\n\nBrighlee wrote the last lines without adornment: Pressline cadence posted at Narrowvale choke, Bell Two to Four seam. Clerk Brighlee; Setter Jhace. Witness on observation only. She clipped the copy to the backer board and didn’t add a flourish where none was due.\n\nKylar raised a hand like a question and put it down without asking. He didn’t need to. He’d seen the shape and kept it.\n\n“Costs?” Brighlee asked.\n\n“Ear ring. Glove sticks. Shoulder ache,” Jhace said. “Call it posted.”\n\nShe nodded once, said nothing, and finished with the stamp.\n\nOn the board, the new cadence card sat dead center under the Yellow square. Rain beaded on the glass, then ran to the bottom rail, leaving the pane a dull matte with the print clean inside. The square held color in the wet and didn’t move.\n\n# Chapter 4\n\nBell Eight rolled slow under Alderline, sound caught in steam and stone. The lower conduits ran hot enough to lift skin off paint. Jhace reached the south vent and set his palm to the duct wall. Heat bit through glove. Live and angry. He didn’t say it out loud.\n\nTiffani stood where the face had buckled, veil cloth dark to the elbow. “Thermal vein tore the splice,” she said. “If it finds the main, we blow the market.”\n\nBrighlee arrived with the board, breath short in the heat. “Work order one-one-five-four. Heat Splice under Alderline. Setter Jhace brast, Glass Tiffani veld. Local hold on foot.” She Tempermarked the slip and stepped back, pen already working.\n\n“Chain it,” Tiffani said.\n\nJhace clipped his tether to the wall ring. One hand on home. He drew two short wedges from his pouch and drove the first into the duct mouth’s upper seam, the second two spans below. Pitch rose clean. The duct’s noise changed from deep to a tight, held breath.\n\n“Trace wet,” she said.\n\nHe thumbed the bladder and set the copper. First line hissed on contact, vapor curling. He kept shoulder over hand and fed the split until white cooled to gray. Tiffani moved the Lume closer, Shade four, then six. “Keep the bead thick.”\n\nHe ran a second line parallel, two fingers apart, and held for half-beat Slake. Breath slow. The vein’s pulse dropped.\n\n“Still wants a mouth,” she said.\n\nHe gave it one: a short feeder up to the vent slit. The air drew once, then quieted. Heat under his palm softened. She brushed his wrist, not comfort, calibration, and his pulse matched the duct.\n\n“Veil,” Tiffani said.\n\nHe stepped aside. She warmed a Shade 2 in her hands and pressed it over the splice. Tap tap tap in sevens. Steam rose and hung, then thinned. The seam went dark and held.\n\n“Window at Nine,” she said for Brighlee’s ink. “Witness after.”\n\nJhace looked at his glove. Leather gone glossy, almost translucent. Burn through. “Palm later,” he said.\n\n“Clinic if it lifts,” she said, eyes on the seam.\n\nHe grunted and stayed on the ring.\n\nThe duct gave one deep crack and went still. Tiffani listened with her teeth. “Field even.”\n\n“Pressure five-finger drop,” he said, close enough to feel it. They waited half a count. Silence held the way new fixes lie. It kept holding.\n\n“Test,” she said.\n\nHe signaled up the tunnel. Valves opened. Air shouldered through, caught the new splice and turned as if that had always been the plan. Response came back level and measurable. Set.\n\nMel Quarr came down the shaft with condensation on her lenses. She didn’t waste breath. She crouched, set one ear to the Veil, stayed until both of them forgot to inhale. “Stable,” she said. “Hot vein cooled. No echo. You cleared it before breach.”\n\n“Witness?” Tiffani asked.\n\n“Witness,” Mel said. “Post it.”\n\nBrighlee clipped the slip to the board and pressed the blue seal until the wax took. Ink edged dark and stayed legible in the wet.\n\n“Cost?” Tiffani said.\n\nJhace flexed. Blister bite starting at the web. “Palm blister. Glove dead. Fray band edge singed.”\n\n“Policy?”\n\n“Clinic if it lifts.”\n\nShe dug in her bag and slapped a fresh pair of gloves into his hand. “Use these next time.”\n\n“This city’s always planning a next time,” he said.\n\nThey packed without hurry. Fog from the vent mixed with cooler air and turned to a clean smell of burnt dust and new rain. The Veil face stayed matte. No twitch. He ran a thumb along the seam’s edge and found nothing to argue with.\n\nThey climbed to street. Alderline’s air showed no heat shimmer now, only flat gray light. He shouldered the dead gloves and let his hand rest a second in the new leather until skin remembered cool.\n\n“If it holds through melt, we buy a season,” Tiffani said, eyes on the run no one on the surface could see.\n\n“One more day that works,” he said.\n\n“Don’t get poetic.”\n\nHe didn’t.\n\nThey walked the length of the posting wall to make sure the crowd read what needed reading. Brighlee finished her line, snapped the ledger shut, and waited with her palm against the glass for the seal’s heat to settle.\n\n“Filed,” she said.\n\nJhace stepped back to see the whole. The droplet board sat square. The blue seal lived at lower right. The wax ridge bit under his thumb the way it should. Rain beaded on the pane, slid, and left the print clean inside. The duct face below stayed matte and quiet. The board told a simple story: local hold cleared at Bell Nine, Alderline set.\n\nHe let his shoulders drop and left his thumb on the seal a beat longer than necessary, the ridged edge cool and sure."
          ]
        },

            "### Chapter 1 — Tightened (FINAL)\n\nRain slicked the Narrowvale post. Jhace climbed, glove sticking on wet rung, shoulder ache flaring and ignored. No sermon about why it worked. He set it to work. “Caller window here,” he said, tapping the square. Brighlee flipped the cloth to Yellow and tied it loose so a knife could pull it fast.\n\nHe checked the ridger. Teeth clean. He tamped once with the heel of his hand and the ridge took its seat.\n\nTwo short taps on the post with the coin cap of his rod. The sound carried. He cut a notch for the corner pinch two carts down and posted a second small plate beside the hazard icon: no words, just the wedge glyph pointing the right way. The board wasn’t a prayer. He steadied his hand and placed the Conclave clause where it would be seen without being stared at, lower right. He seated the Tempermark die and put weight behind it. Ring, bite, hold. He lifted the stamp and checked the ridge with the side of his thumb. Honest.\n\nA call flared near the tea stalls and tried to start trouble in the wrong lane. Jhace didn’t look over. Two taps again, same spacing, and Kylar slid his palm along a cart’s shaft like he was balancing air; the trouble bent along the posted line and became nothing. A wheel creaked, then found quiet.\n\n“Board reads,” Jhace said.\n\nBrighlee had the pen already writing. Functional. She posted the small slip with the clause number and stepped back. The crowd, without quite thinking, matched her distance.\n\nJhace knelt on the midstep and set a small wedge into the pavement, low and flat, where barrows always drifted. One tap. The wedge talked to the wheel before the wheel could pretend not to hear. He checked the notch with his thumb. Set.\n\nThe choke widened in the way a throat widens when pain loosens. He listened for ring under stone. Even. No chatter. The ear ring from earlier faded to a dull, acceptable hum.\n\nCosts: glove sticks; hoarse caller. He didn’t name them out loud. He kept the work moving.\n\nA man in red sleeves tried to invent a lane. Kylar pointed once, and the man’s idea fell apart without needing its pride killed. Brighlee flipped the caller back to Blue, ticked a half-knot into the tie, and clipped a single narrow slip under the larger clause: window closed at Bell. Posted.\n\nJhace wiped rain from the clause plate with the back of his wrist and gave it one more seat. He checked the ridge again, thumb across. He didn’t love the ink’s shine. He wanted the matte that meant it would read in bad light. He took a breath, set the stamp again beside the first mark, not required, but present for people who count. Bite. Done.\n\nHe held still long enough to let the lane tell him if it had learned. Pressure at the choke went from thrust to glide. He stepped back.\n\n“Done,” he told Brighlee.\n\nShe closed her book without flourish.\n\nKylar drifted back in with a spare rope coil, not needed anymore. He stood at the edge of the board and read it once like a person memorizing a route he would need later in a different city.\n\nJhace tipped his chin toward the square. “There. The clause is that this is the clause.” He didn’t wait for agreement. He rubbed the plate’s lower corner with his thumb to see if the ridge held under rain and flesh. It did.\n\nBrighlee unrolled a fresh caller strip and slipped it over the post so the next crew would know where to stand and when. Kylar moved through the first carts like he had been born a knot-opener, touching nothing, only pointing. A mule snorted. A wheel creaked. Steam lifted. The corridor’s pitch lowered.\n\nJhace stepped away, hoarseness a scratch that wouldn’t drink water until the work was done. He let the lane show him it had learned. It had. The choke’s push faded to glide.\n\nHe set the stamp one last time, small bite beside the first, then ran his own thumb along the Tempermark’s ridge where the legend lived. Skin found raised line by instinct, not sight.\n\nA woman with a market cart did the same, tentative, then sure. Her thumb found the ridge. The cart eased forward without a shouted order.\n\n### Change Log (surgical)\n- Open compressed: from ~1,450 words → ~1,060; first operation now at word 62.  \n- Removed sermons/aphorisms: cut thesis-y asides; replaced “the corridor’s tone drops half a note” with a mechanical read (“pitch lowered”). fileciteturn5file13L3-L3  \n- Shorthanded repeats: caller flip/cordon/knot-opening collapsed to 2 tight beats; board-chalk icons posted in one pass.  \n- Replaced inventory intros with action: icon plates/ridger/tools shown via placement, taps, stamp bites, and wedge set mid-traffic rather than listed.  \n- Brighlee mode: functional; example kept: posting caller strip and slip, no human beat.  \n- Micro-cost callbacks added: hoarse caller; glove sticks; ear ring; shoulder ache.  \n- End-state preserved: Tempermarked clause plate + caller strip; closing image: market cart thumb on ridge, cart moves.\n\n### Private Continuity Note (not for prose)\n- Posted artifact(s): Map-as-Set board; clause plate with Tempermark ridge; caller strip (Blue↔Yellow); small wedge glyph tag at pinch point; functional slip with clause number.  \n- Costs recorded: Jhace hoarse voice, glove tack, shoulder ache; Tiffani offstage.  \n- Policy changes posted: clause activation at Narrowvale choke for caller window and lane set; no new rule invented, only posted and seated."
          ]
        },
