          "result": "# 【0†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file0\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Bell Ten and a half\\! Setter Jhace brast, Glass Tiffani veld—Lockfall emergency\\!”  \n[L2] “Post later\\!” Tiffani said. “Witness first\\!”  \n[L3] Mel Quarr stumbled in from the north end, coat dripping. “You’re lucky I was on the bridge,” she said.  \n[L4] “Get down here,” Jhace said. “We need your ear.”  \n[L5] Mel crouched, pressed her head to the lock seam. Her jaw tightened. “Still movement.”  \n[L6] “We bled it\\!”  \n[L7] “Not enough.” She moved her hand lower, searching. “There’s another line.”  \n[L8] Tiffani cursed. “The feeder vein.”  \n[L9] Jhace turned, searching the wall through steam. “Where?”  \n[L10] “Under you.”  \n[L11] He knelt, groped through the flood, found the hairline crack near his boot. Warm water bubbled through. He shoved the copper nib into it, let the bladder feed until the hiss died.  \n[L12] “Now?” he said.  \n[L13] Mel stayed still for a long count. Then she looked up, doubtful. “Maybe.”  \n[L14] “Maybe doesn’t clear it,” Tiffani said.  \n[L15] Mel pressed her ear again, eyes shut. Silence. “Now.”  \n[L16] Jhace slumped against the wall. The water around his knees had gone still, the kind of stillness that made sound travel.  \n[L17] Brighlee wiped water from her ledger with the side of her sleeve. “Lockfall at Greymantle Cut contained. Local hold to remain partial until tomorrow inspection.”  \n[L18] “Post it,” Tiffani said.  \n[L19] Brighlee clipped the slip to the drenched board. The ink ran but stayed legible.  \n[L20] Jhace peeled his glove. His palm was split across the web between thumb and forefinger, blood mixing with gray water.  \n[L21] “Cost?” she asked.  \n[L22] He flexed the hand; skin pulled. “Grip’s gone for a day.”  \n[L23] “Clinic,” she said.  \n[L24] “I said a day, not a week.”  \n[L25] “Policy doesn’t care what you said.”  \n[L26] He laughed once.  \n[L27] The lock groaned, then quieted. Mel stood, brushing grit from her coat. “You held it,” she said. “Don’t expect thanks.”  \n[L28] “Wouldn’t know what to do with it,” Jhace said.  \n[L29] She almost smiled. “Good. You’ll get another call before dawn.”  \n[L30] She left up the wet stairs, boots leaving perfect prints.  \n[L31] Tiffani leaned on the gate, breathing hard. “You almost drowned.”  \n[L32] He nodded. “Almost fixed it, too.”  \n[L33] Her hand found his wrist, still wet. “Stay,” she said.  \n[L34] He did.  \n[L35] They stood until the canal calmed and the sound of the Bells faded under the water’s slow rise.\n[L36] \n[L37] ## **Chapter 6: Corlexi’s Fire (Interlude)**\n[L38] \n[L39] Bell Twelve rolled flat over the roofs, muffled by mist and smoke. Corlexi stood in the market square with both hands in the ash bin, pulling placards out one by one. Each card bore the old single‑brace symbol: two wedges facing inward, out of date by one Bell. Every lock failure that week had carried that mark. Policy changed this morning. Someone had to burn the proof.  \n[L40] He held the first card by its corner. The wax Tempermark bled blue when the heat touched it, curling into itself like muscle recoiling. The stamp read Greymantle Cut—Work Order 2043\\. He remembered the red handprint rumor before he saw the stain burned into the ink edge.\n# 【1†Standards in Motion.md†file_0000000077ac61f5865c2e2818920c23】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Standards in Motion.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file1\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Tiffani felt the cost in her middle, not as pain, just as a lack that pulled her tall. She placed the last of the clean vials in a neat row and stepped back so the room could show her if it had learned anything. It had. No hands drifted toward the reversed‑seal crate. Trainees looked at the chart before asking out loud. Salee’s ledger squared up with the shelf’s new shape.\n[L2] \n[L3] “Chart reads,” Salee said. She approved with her chin. “Lot lives in the open. We’re not short. We’re honest.” She picked up the ration slips and posted them above the chart in a V shape that made a corridor of paper.\n[L4] \n[L5] Tiffani wiped the pane, cleaned the chalks, rolled them back into their leather, then took the rejected vial from the tray and set it into the burn dish to break the habit of reaching for shiny things. The flame licked the lip; the oil guttered and turned to a thin string of smoke that smelled like nothing at all, which is the smell of a lie ending.\n[L6] \n[L7] She let herself lean one shoulder into the cool stone beside the shelf for three breaths. Heat left her hands and went into the wall where it wouldn’t be wasted. The room steadied at a low hum. Outside, a bell note bent and straightened.\n[L8] \n[L9] She stepped back to the wall for the last small job. With the ridger, she bit a second patch into the chart’s **test here** square so fingers could compare the ridge to the chalk’s drag. She pressed the Conclave stamp lightly again, not to double it, only to line it up so it would be obvious if anyone tried to lift the chart and replace it.\n[L10] \n[L11] The Annex felt correct. Stock updated. Chart posted. Crate red‑slashed. She picked up a clear vial from **L‑73** and held it to the lamp. The liquid inside showed a clean, slow line around its shoulder. Beside it on the lid of **L‑72**, the red‑slashed square flagged the off‑color seal without a speech. The two sat in the same light, one quiet and right, the other refusing to be handled.\n[L12] \n[L13] # **Chapter 7: Batch Recall**\n[L14] \n[L15] Jhace hit the Narrowvale choke at Bell Two‑to‑Four seam with the recall bundle under his arm and Chloen already chalking the stone. Rain had stopped, but the air still tasted like a cooled pan. On the posting wall, Tiffani’s warning card about counterfeits sat where it should, and Salee’s oil chart was copied small in a corner by someone’s careful hand. L‑72 lived on that chart with a red corner slash. Today, that slash would get teeth.\n[L16] \n[L17] “Lot and pass,” Chloen said, soft. She had the stencils for **L** and **P** ready, the little quill‑square stamp tucked under her thumb. Kylar uncoiled the quarantine cord like it was a live snake that respected him.\n[L18] \n[L19] A boy with a hand‑barrow pushed through where he shouldn’t and stopped, reading the arrows late. Vendors muttered about time and fees. Steam curled from a kettle and drew a map of impatience across the lane. Jhace set the recall bundle on the shelf, cracked it open, and found the red slash placards stacked tidy as tiles. He tapped his calf against the post to wake the muscle. Long day ahead.\n[L20] \n[L21] “Start at south end,” he said. “Red slash on both walls. Cord between. Caller posts swing wide.” His throat still held sand from yesterday’s taps; the rasp lived there like a minor debt.\n# 【2†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file2\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Bell Three at the Clinic rang like the edge of a cool bowl. Salee stood behind the counter with a stack of blue slips and a stamp that smelled faintly of lemon oil. Workers came in pairs or alone, shoulders straight until they saw the color and then they sagged.  \n[L2] “Name,” she said to the first.  \n[L3] “Jhace brast,” Brighlee answered for him, pushing him forward by the elbow, not looking at his face like dignity required.  \n[L4] Salee didn’t blink. “Burn, persistent. Knee strain.” She wrote fast, neat. “Rest prescription: one Bell immediate, two after shift. Paid.”  \n[L5] Jhace started to object. Salee slid the slip into his hand without touching his skin. “Policy,” she said. “Posted.”  \n[L6] He looked at the wall where the placard hung beside the clinic door. The words were plain: Mandatory rest after consecutive shifts exceeding four Bells. Paid. Enforcement: Clinic stamp overrides work order.  \n[L7] Tiffani stood to the side, color back in her face but eyes ringed. Salee wrote her a second slip: Dehydration, Shade error corrected. Rest: one Bell. Paid.  \n[L8] A foreman edged up, hat in hands. “We’re light on crew.”  \n[L9] Salee stamped a third slip and handed it to him. “Then reschedule. That’s the point.”  \n[L10] He opened his mouth. Closed it. Took the paper.  \n[L11] Mel Quarr signed the margin of the new placard with a small, precise hand. “Witness,” she said, not loud, but no one missed it.  \n[L12] Corlexi passed through with a satchel of old clinic forms headed for the burn barrel. He touched the edge of the new sign like it might be hot. “About time,” he said.  \n[L13] Mobel from the Mercers’ Table arrived with a tin of boiled sweets and set them on the counter. “For compliance,” he said. “Not as bribe.”  \n[L14] Salee cracked a smile with one side of her mouth. “Accepted.”  \n[L15] She stamped without pause for an hour. The counter filled with the small sounds of paper, the big silence of people not arguing. At the half-Bell, she replaced the ink pad with a fresh one and posted the second part of the policy beneath the first: Repeat offenders: mandatory two-Bell rest. Dock illegal.  \n[L16] A boy from the Underface brought his mother’s note: Breath short in cold. Salee stamped that too, then handed the boy a sweet and a folded copy of the rest placard. “Post this at her workroom,” she said. “High where she can see it.”  \n[L17] He nodded, small and serious.  \n[L18] When the Bell chimed the quarter, Tiffani and Jhace sat side by side on a bench under the window. They didn’t speak. Their shoulders dropped at the same moment like a weight setting. Salee watched without looking like she was watching and decided not to put that on any form.  \n[L19] She finished the stack, wiped the stamp clean, and posted the day’s count on a small slate: Rest slips issued: 38\\. Refusals: 0\\.  \n[L20] Outside the clinic, the city sounded exactly the same as before. Inside, it didn’t.  \n[L21] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L22] \n[L23] ## **Chapter 15: Small Fix at Lantern Stairs**\n[L24] \n[L25] Bell Six came soft along Lantern Stairs, filtered through laundry lines and morning cook smoke. The call was a polite one: minor creak at the mid-landing brace. No one rushed. They went anyway.\n# 【3†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file3\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Tiffani held up the torn piece. “Take it back.”  \n[L2] “I’m paid to deliver to store.”  \n[L3] “Then you can tell them you weren’t allowed.”  \n[L4] He glanced at Jhace. Jhace stared at the cart until the man glanced away. “Fine,” the teamster muttered, and turned the cart.  \n[L5] They finished the rounds just as Mel Quarr caught them at the seventh corner. “You’re walking a pattern,” she said.  \n[L6] “Tainted Veil lot,” Tiffani said, handing her the scrap. “L/P–43. We replaced six. Two more not yet laid, in a cart headed back.”  \n[L7] Mel rolled a bit of the torn skin between her fingers. It turned to crumbs. “I can’t clear what I didn’t see fail.”  \n[L8] “Then witness the replacements,” Jhace said. “Or come back tomorrow to a crack.”  \n[L9] Mel met his eyes a long count, then nodded. “Show me one.”  \n[L10] They took her to the bubbled Veil on the lower lip. Tiffani lifted it clean with a knife, exposing the good trace below.  \n[L11] “Plate’s honest,” Jhace said, and drew a short wet to refresh the line. Steam flicked, then went still.  \n[L12] Tiffani laid a Shade 1 from a known good lot. Tap, tap, seven per edge.  \n[L13] Mel pressed her palm to the seal and stayed still. “This holds,” she said. “Post replacements for all L/P–43 placed within the last two Bells. I’ll sign.”  \n[L14] Brighlee Tempermarked the note and clipped it beside the hatch. “Lot quarantine,” she said under her breath, like the words had their own weight.  \n[L15] “Cost?” Tiffani asked Jhace as they packed.  \n[L16] He flexed his wrapped hand. Blood had dried brown along one edge. “Grip’s back. Knee’s gone.”  \n[L17] “Clinic.”  \n[L18] “Policy.”  \n[L19] They left the sump cleaner than they found it and walked back into the Underface where steam threw long, soft shadows. By Bell Three, the rumor had reached the Crown. Runners began pulling L/P–43 from Conclave shelves without waiting for orders.  \n[L20] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L21] \n[L22] ## **Chapter 12: Skew Line at Wains Cut**\n[L23] \n[L24] Bell Seven carried dust. The Cut at Wains threw noise in strange directions, the kind that hid faults until they were underfoot. Tiffani and Jhace made the east stair in twenty-seven breaths, short and counted, then stopped at a joint where the floor plates met the wall collar. The line ran straight for two spans and then drifted a hair toward the drain.  \n[L25] “Skew,” Jhace said. “Someone chased a leak with dry trace.”  \n[L26] Tiffani crouched, Lume low. The line was pretty and wrong—neat, thin, and shiny. “Whitequench’s cousin,” she said. “Looks good. Lies.”  \n[L27] Brighlee arrived with the ledger already open. “Work order one-two-one-eight. Line correction, Wains Cut. Local hold on foot.” Functional again; no garnish.  \n[L28] A supervisor from the Cut eased close, hands empty, eyes hungry. “We need it open,” he said. “Our crew kept it neat.”  \n[L29] “Neat isn’t safe,” Tiffani said. “Back two meters.”  \n[L30] He didn’t move. Jhace did, a half-step that put his shoulder between Tiffani and the man. The supervisor took the hint and faded.\n# 【4†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file4\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The supervisor took the hint and faded.  \n[L2] “Angle?” Jhace asked.  \n[L3] “Two degrees toward drain for three spans,” Tiffani said, tapping chalk marks. “Then straight to the corner. We pull the lie and give it a path.”  \n[L4] He set two wedges lightly to keep the plate from talking while they corrected the line. “Half-beat Slake,” he said, more to himself than to her.  \n[L5] She cut the wet into the white. The false line lifted like shed skin when the real pressure touched it. It came away in narrow curls that stank faint of solvent. “Shop trace,” she said. “They painted it.”  \n[L6] Jhace swore. “Who?”  \n[L7] “Same lot mark from the Veil recall,” Brighlee said without looking up. “Lonce & Parr sells more than skins.”  \n[L8] Tiffani nodded once, tight. “We post it later. Fix it now.”  \n[L9] She traced the true line with a bead heavy enough to sink into the plate’s shallow grain. Jhace fed from his bladder, steady. They Slaked on the half-beat, then bridged the turn with a small counterline to keep the path honest.  \n[L10] “Pulse?” he asked.  \n[L11] She listened, mouth open. “Level.”  \n[L12] “Veil?”  \n[L13] She warmed a Shade 1 from a Crown lot that had passed her knife test twice this morning and pressed it down. Tap, tap. The seal went dull.  \n[L14] The supervisor edged closer again. “So we’re clear?”  \n[L15] “No,” Tiffani said, not looking up. “We’re safe. Clear when the witness signs.”  \n[L16] Mel Quarr came on the last word as if conjured by impatience. She crouched, laid her palm to the line, and went still. Her nose wrinkled. “I hate the smell of shop trace.”  \n[L17] Tiffani held up a curl. “We’re posting a ban.”  \n[L18] “Good,” Mel said. “This holds.” She wrote the signature on Brighlee’s page. “Post your quarantine across product, not lot. Lonce & Parr doesn’t get to sell glue to hold down lies.”  \n[L19] The supervisor tried again. “We needed the lane.”  \n[L20] “You need a city,” Jhace said. “This keeps it.”  \n[L21] Mel stood. “Send a runner to Mercers’ Table. Mobel likes melting false measures.”  \n[L22] “Already on the page,” Brighlee said, clipping the SHOP TRACE BAN beside the hatch. Hands paused, then moved.  \n[L23] “Cost?” Tiffani asked.  \n[L24] Jhace flexed his hand. “Grip back. Knee worse.”  \n[L25] “Clinic after Bell Nine,” Mel said. “I’ll write the slip so you can’t argue.”  \n[L26] He didn’t try.  \n[L27] They cleared the hold. The Cut’s supervisor kept his eyes on his boots while the first few workers stepped past the post, glancing down at the new, dull line as if it might speak.  \n[L28] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L29] \n[L30] ## **Chapter 13: Thirteen Corners**\n[L31] \n[L32] Bell Eleven began with a miscount. The Crown clock stuttered on the second note, and in the Underface three corners away, a drip slowed exactly in time. Jhace didn’t hear any of that, but he felt the city tighten, the way steel answers steel.  \n[L33] The first call hit Brighlee’s board as a smear: Plate lift at Farwell. Before she finished posting, a runner brought Veil peel at Rookwell. Then Coupler chatter at Gray Arch. The paper stacked crooked.\n# 【5†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file5\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Tiffani checked feedback. “Trace reads steady. If it fails, it’ll do it politely.”  \n[L2] “Polite’s new.”  \n[L3] “Maybe the city’s learning.”  \n[L4] He sat back, wiping sweat with his good sleeve. “You’re shaking.”  \n[L5] “So are you.”  \n[L6] “Then we match.”  \n[L7] “Don’t make poetry out of exhaustion.”  \n[L8] He didn’t.  \n[L9] Mel Quarr appeared at the top of the ramp, as tired as the rest. “Report.”  \n[L10] “Minor leak, sealed,” Tiffani said. “No hold. Feed equalized.”  \n[L11] Mel knelt, pressed her hand to the plate. “Holding.” She looked at Jhace’s arm. “You working injured again?”  \n[L12] “Clinic after shift.”  \n[L13] “Clinic now. I’ll send a slip.”  \n[L14] Brighlee pinned the form to the board. “Work order four‑eight‑one complete. Bell Eleven.”  \n[L15] “Costs?” Mel asked.  \n[L16] “Burn reopening,” Jhace said.  \n[L17] “Dehydration,” Tiffani added.  \n[L18] Mel wrote both. “Clinic, mandatory.”  \n[L19] They didn’t argue.  \n[L20] When the others left, Jhace stayed a minute longer. The span carried a steady pulse under fog.  \n[L21] Tiffani leaned against the rail beside him. “You’re bleeding through the wrap.”  \n[L22] A small patch of red showed. “Proof I’m alive.”  \n[L23] She leaned her head against the same rail. “Alive’s overrated.” Her shoulder brushed his. His tremor eased.  \n[L24] They left the Lume burning low on the post, light catching the still‑wet line like a thin scar that refused to brighten.  \n[L25] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L26] \n[L27] ## **Chapter 10: Mobel at the Table (Interlude)**\n[L28] \n[L29] Bell Two chimed through the Mercers’ Quarter, each note sliding through the lattice rafters above the weighing floor. Mobel had been there since dawn, coat off, sleeves rolled, the kind of tired that doesn’t show until it stops moving.  \n[L30] The scales in front of him were old Crown brass, polished to dull gold. They didn’t lie often, but they could be coaxed. The night auditors had caught three weight sets marked light in the middle coin. Not enough to hang a merchant, enough to make the Conclave twitch.  \n[L31] He placed one suspect weight on the pan, thumbed the notch along its rim. “Filed too deep,” he said to his clerk. “Half‑grain shave.”  \n[L32] The clerk squinted. “Still legal?”  \n[L33] Mobel didn’t answer. He flicked the weight off the pan. It hit the floor with a hollow ring. “Not when it starts breeding.”  \n[L34] The smell of old oil filled the air as apprentices cleaned their scales. Every trade table in the room carried its own seal—the droplet for Couriers, the hammer for Forgewrights, the two crossed wedges newly added for Locks and Loads. That mark had cost Jhace a burn and half the city a rulebook rewrite.  \n[L35] He checked the next weight. The brass surface bore faint scrape lines, deliberate. He turned it over and read the faint initials carved into the base. “Harnet.”  \n[L36] The clerk’s eyes widened. “He’s senior.”  \n[L37] “Was,” Mobel said. “Post the charge.”  \n[L38] The clerk hesitated. “Sir, it’s only half a grain—”  \n[L39] Mobel’s voice stayed even. “Half a grain turns into famine when multiplied. Post it.”  \n[L40] The clerk went pale but moved, writing the notice on a strip of wax paper.\n# 【6†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file6\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] MIRECOURT Tiffani found only fog. The leak they’d fixed stayed fixed. She wrote steady and turned.  \n[L2] GREYMANTLE CUT Jhace waited at the last step and listened. The lock below moved water in a slow, obedient draw. He didn’t go down. He didn’t need to.  \n[L3] The round took three Bells. They met again where they’d started, at Brighlee’s board. The paper stack had become a neat column with times and marks.  \n[L4] “Count?” Jhace asked.  \n[L5] “Thirteen corners,” Brighlee said. “No failures.” Her fingers were stained gray from page edges. “One junior wrote himself up.”  \n[L6] Tiffani tilted her head toward Gray Arch. “Good.”  \n[L7] Mel Quarr stepped out of the press of bodies, hair stuck to her face, coat open. “I read your marks as I walked,” she said. “This is what it looks like when a city learns.”  \n[L8] Jhace stretched his burned arm with slow care. “Or when it chooses not to forget.”  \n[L9] Mel’s mouth twitched. “Don’t get poetic.”  \n[L10] He didn’t.  \n[L11] She signed the top sheet without kneeling this time. “I’ll witness the round as a set. Post the list.”  \n[L12] Tiffani wiped her knife. “Costs?”  \n[L13] Jhace looked at his hand. The split had closed, but the skin was thick and new. “Grip returning. Knee still wrong.”  \n[L14] “Dry mouth,” Tiffani said. “Low sugar.”  \n[L15] “Clinic,” Mel said. “Both of you. Salee’s posted new slips.”  \n[L16] They didn’t argue.  \n[L17] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L18] \n[L19] ## **Chapter 14: Salee’s Slip (Interlude)**\n[L20] \n[L21] Bell Three at the Clinic rang like the edge of a cool bowl. Salee stood behind the counter with a stack of blue slips and a stamp that smelled faintly of lemon oil. Workers came in pairs or alone, shoulders straight until they saw the color and then they sagged.  \n[L22] “Name,” she said to the first.  \n[L23] “Jhace brast,” Brighlee answered for him, pushing him forward by the elbow, not looking at his face like dignity required.  \n[L24] Salee didn’t blink. “Burn, persistent. Knee strain.” She wrote fast, neat. “Rest prescription: one Bell immediate, two after shift. Paid.”  \n[L25] Jhace started to object. Salee slid the slip into his hand without touching his skin. “Policy,” she said. “Posted.”  \n[L26] He looked at the wall where the placard hung beside the clinic door. The words were plain: Mandatory rest after consecutive shifts exceeding four Bells. Paid. Enforcement: Clinic stamp overrides work order.  \n[L27] Tiffani stood to the side, color back in her face but eyes ringed. Salee wrote her a second slip: Dehydration, Shade error corrected. Rest: one Bell. Paid.  \n[L28] A foreman edged up, hat in hands. “We’re light on crew.”  \n[L29] Salee stamped a third slip and handed it to him. “Then reschedule. That’s the point.”  \n[L30] He opened his mouth. Closed it. Took the paper.  \n[L31] Mel Quarr signed the margin of the new placard with a small, precise hand. “Witness,” she said, not loud, but no one missed it.  \n[L32] Corlexi passed through with a satchel of old clinic forms headed for the burn barrel. He touched the edge of the new sign like it might be hot.\n# 【7†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file7\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “I hate the smell of shop trace.”  \n[L2] Tiffani held up a curl. “We’re posting a ban.”  \n[L3] “Good,” Mel said. “This holds.” She wrote the signature on Brighlee’s page. “Post your quarantine across product, not lot. Lonce & Parr doesn’t get to sell glue to hold down lies.”  \n[L4] The supervisor tried again. “We needed the lane.”  \n[L5] “You need a city,” Jhace said. “This keeps it.”  \n[L6] Mel stood. “Send a runner to Mercers’ Table. Mobel likes melting false measures.”  \n[L7] “Already on the page,” Brighlee said, clipping the SHOP TRACE BAN beside the hatch. Hands paused, then moved.  \n[L8] “Cost?” Tiffani asked.  \n[L9] Jhace flexed his hand. “Grip back. Knee worse.”  \n[L10] “Clinic after Bell Nine,” Mel said. “I’ll write the slip so you can’t argue.”  \n[L11] He didn’t try.  \n[L12] They cleared the hold. The Cut’s supervisor kept his eyes on his boots while the first few workers stepped past the post, glancing down at the new, dull line as if it might speak.  \n[L13] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L14] \n[L15] ## **Chapter 13: Thirteen Corners**\n[L16] \n[L17] Bell Eleven began with a miscount. The Crown clock stuttered on the second note, and in the Underface three corners away, a drip slowed exactly in time. Jhace didn’t hear any of that, but he felt the city tighten, the way steel answers steel.  \n[L18] The first call hit Brighlee’s board as a smear: Plate lift at Farwell. Before she finished posting, a runner brought Veil peel at Rookwell. Then Coupler chatter at Gray Arch. The paper stacked crooked. She didn’t panic. She sorted. “You split,” she said. “Tiffani north. Jhace west.”  \n[L19] They didn’t argue. He took the west bag and ran.  \n[L20] FARWELL Two wedges already in, wrong places, a crewman white-faced and sweating. “It started to whine—”  \n[L21] “Don’t use that field,” Jhace said. He shoved the man back with his hip, set a proper wedge at the shoulder, low and flat. The plate’s flutter dropped. He traced a short wet from the bow to the anchor, Slaked on the half, and nodded. “Now breathe.” He was gone before thanks could form.  \n[L22] ROOKWELL Tiffani arrived to find the Veil lifting in three fingers along a turn. Lot stamp: not L/P–43. Something else. She pried the corner, found grit under the lip—poor clean. “Who set this?” she asked. No one answered. She fixed it anyway: lift, wipe, wet, seal. She left the torn skin on the post where shame could see it.  \n[L23] GRAY ARCH Jhace hit the coupler at a fast jog and felt the chatter through his teeth. He didn’t wait for light. He laid both palms, counted the pulse, then drove a brace rod through the flange before he took tools out of the bag. When he finally looked up, a junior from the night shift was trying to look small. “I tightened to proud,” the kid said.  \n[L24] Jhace didn’t waste words. He loosened, reset, Slaked. “Watch,” he said. The kid did.  \n[L25] BRIDGEGATE SIDE RUN Tiffani found a drip at the side run that wasn’t a leak.\n# 【8†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file8\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Cost?” she asked.  \n[L2] He flexed the hand; skin pulled. “Grip’s gone for a day.”  \n[L3] “Clinic,” she said.  \n[L4] “I said a day, not a week.”  \n[L5] “Policy doesn’t care what you said.”  \n[L6] He laughed once.  \n[L7] The lock groaned, then quieted. Mel stood, brushing grit from her coat. “You held it,” she said. “Don’t expect thanks.”  \n[L8] “Wouldn’t know what to do with it,” Jhace said.  \n[L9] She almost smiled. “Good. You’ll get another call before dawn.”  \n[L10] She left up the wet stairs, boots leaving perfect prints.  \n[L11] Tiffani leaned on the gate, breathing hard. “You almost drowned.”  \n[L12] He nodded. “Almost fixed it, too.”  \n[L13] Her hand found his wrist, still wet. “Stay,” she said.  \n[L14] He did.  \n[L15] They stood until the canal calmed and the sound of the Bells faded under the water’s slow rise.\n[L16] \n[L17] ## **Chapter 6: Corlexi’s Fire (Interlude)**\n[L18] \n[L19] Bell Twelve rolled flat over the roofs, muffled by mist and smoke. Corlexi stood in the market square with both hands in the ash bin, pulling placards out one by one. Each card bore the old single‑brace symbol: two wedges facing inward, out of date by one Bell. Every lock failure that week had carried that mark. Policy changed this morning. Someone had to burn the proof.  \n[L20] He held the first card by its corner. The wax Tempermark bled blue when the heat touched it, curling into itself like muscle recoiling. The stamp read Greymantle Cut—Work Order 2043\\. He remembered the red handprint rumor before he saw the stain burned into the ink edge. “Lucky he didn’t drown,” he muttered, and dropped it into the flame.  \n[L21] A group of apprentices watched from the steps, carrying fresh boards. They whispered to each other, eyes on the burn box. “Double brace rule?” one asked.  \n[L22] Corlexi didn’t look up. “Double brace rule. Within ten rounds.”  \n[L23] The fire spat once. Wax smell thickened. The second placard carried a name he knew—Maren hilt, a good setter who’d cracked a knee when a brace failed two months ago. He held it longer before letting it fall.  \n[L24] On the far side of the square, the Couriers’ Bell office was already laying out the new boards. Ink crews worked off a folding table, stamping wedge glyphs two‑by‑two, copper heads hot enough to hiss when they touched wood. The pattern had changed overnight: old inward braces replaced by paired diagonals, more metal, more safety.  \n[L25] “Quicker this way?” an apprentice asked.  \n[L26] “No,” Corlexi said. “Slower, safer.”  \n[L27] He fed the third placard into the bin. The crowd near the food stalls had gone quiet, watching the smoke. It wasn’t ceremony; it was relief disguised as boredom.  \n[L28] A Conclave clerk crossed the square with a basket of new seals. “Posting priority corners first,” he said, voice careful. “The Conclave wants public compliance before dusk.”  \n[L29] Corlexi wiped sweat from his cheek with the back of his arm. “They always do.”  \n[L30] The clerk hesitated, holding a placard halfway. “You were there for Lowcourt, right?”  \n[L31] “Doesn’t matter where I was.” He took the placard and set it into the fire. “Now I’m here.”  \n[L32] The last of the old signs turned to smoke. He raked the ashes with a long iron rod until the glyphs broke apart.\n# 【9†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file9\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “A record.” He stirred the molten brass with a rod, watching the color shift from yellow to red to dull silver. “Shows fade. Records stay on the board.”  \n[L2] When the last of the fake weights had gone under, he tilted the crucible, poured the liquid into a flat mold shaped like a wedge. “New symbol,” he said. “Half‑grain truth.”  \n[L3] The clerk stared. “What do we do with it?”  \n[L4] “Post it on the Table.”  \n[L5] They nailed the cooled wedge to the central board between the trade seals, the scarred metal still faintly warm. It read: Mass must match mark. Deviance voids claim.  \n[L6] Mobel looked around the room. No one met his eyes. “Back to work,” he said.  \n[L7] The merchants dispersed. The clatter of weights returned, but softer, careful now.  \n[L8] At the Bell of mid‑morning, the clerk handed him the ledger. “Three confessions filed already,” she said. “Two from vendors who weren’t questioned.”  \n[L9] Mobel nodded, too tired for satisfaction. “Guilt travels faster than ink.”  \n[L10] He sat a while after the room cleared, listening to the slow tick of cooling metal. He wasn’t proud. Just done. He wiped his hands on his coat and left the burned smell for the next shift.  \n[L11] Outside, a new placard hung on the Mercers’ archway—approved by existing rule. It carried Corlexi’s twin‑wedge symbol above the words DOUBLE CHECK. DOUBLE PAY. He smiled once, small, then vanished into the crowd before anyone could mistake it for approval.\n[L12] \n[L13] ## **Chapter 11: Batch Marks at Chalkway Sump**\n[L14] \n[L15] Bell One slid thin along the underpipes. The sump room at Chalkway stank of iron and steeped cloth. Condensation ran down the wall in a slow sheet. Tiffani stood ankle-deep in runoff with the Lume already up, light skimming a seam where yesterday’s repair wore a perfect edge—except the Veil lip had lifted a fingernail on the downstream corner.  \n[L16] “Edge lift,” she said. “No reason for it.”  \n[L17] Jhace pressed a knuckle to the joint. The plate felt cool, honest. “Field’s even.”  \n[L18] She ran a thumbnail under the lifted lip. The skin flexed, then tore clean along its own weave. “Veil delam.”  \n[L19] “Bad batch?”  \n[L20] “Or someone stored it hot.”  \n[L21] Brighlee’s voice echoed from the hatch. “Work order nine-nine-two. Veil failure, Chalkway Sump. Local hold to foot traffic.” She clipped the slip inside the hatch mouth and stayed put. Functional today.  \n[L22] Tiffani peeled another edge. The skin tore with no stretch, like old paper. “Shade mark reads one,” she said, squinting at the faint stamp. “Lot L/P–43.”  \n[L23] “Lonce & Parr,” Jhace said. “Crown supplier.”  \n[L24] She nodded once. “We fix the seam first.”  \n[L25] He set a wedge midline to quiet any wander, then thumbed the bladder and drew a short wet along the seam where the Veil had lifted. Half-beat Slake. Steam rose and vanished. The line took at once. No fault in the plate.  \n[L26] Tiffani warmed a new Shade 1 from her kit—different stamp, a local mark—and laid it in with seven taps per edge. The lip sealed tight, no haze.  \n[L27] “Test,” Jhace said.  \n[L28] She ran the Lume along it. Light came back flat.\n# 【10†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file10\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Post it.”  \n[L2] The clerk went pale but moved, writing the notice on a strip of wax paper. Mobel pulled the ink stamp from his belt, Tempermarked the sheet, and clipped it to the board above the scales. The room stilled. Every merchant in sight turned toward the sound.  \n[L3] “Official?” one called.  \n[L4] “Filed under Bell Two,” Mobel said.  \n[L5] Someone muttered, “Conclave doesn’t usually bother with half‑grains.”  \n[L6] “They do now.”  \n[L7] He reached for the next weight, turned it, sniffed the faint tang of fresh polish. Too clean. He scraped a knife edge across it. Bright line. “Fresh file,” he said.  \n[L8] A Forgewright approached, face dark. “We recalibrate weekly. No harm in keeping tools smooth.”  \n[L9] “Smooth isn’t light,” Mobel said. He balanced the weight against a verified measure. The pan dipped slow, traitorous. “See?”  \n[L10] The forgewright looked away. “Barely a whisper.”  \n[L11] “That whisper just cost you your table.”  \n[L12] He wrote the notice himself: Forgery of official weights, Harnet Forge Cooperative. License revoked pending audit. Seals to be melted public. He signed, Tempermarked, and handed it to the clerk. “Fire the furnace.”  \n[L13] The apprentices dragged a small crucible from the side wall, set it on the coals, pumped the bellows until the air shimmered. The smell of heating metal cut through the damp.  \n[L14] Mobel took the forged weights one by one, dropped them into the pot. They sizzled as moisture burned off, then sagged into liquid. The room went quiet except for the hiss of betrayal cooking.  \n[L15] The Forgewright spoke again, voice thin. “You’re making a show of this.”  \n[L16] “No,” Mobel said. “A record.” He stirred the molten brass with a rod, watching the color shift from yellow to red to dull silver. “Shows fade. Records stay on the board.”  \n[L17] When the last of the fake weights had gone under, he tilted the crucible, poured the liquid into a flat mold shaped like a wedge. “New symbol,” he said. “Half‑grain truth.”  \n[L18] The clerk stared. “What do we do with it?”  \n[L19] “Post it on the Table.”  \n[L20] They nailed the cooled wedge to the central board between the trade seals, the scarred metal still faintly warm. It read: Mass must match mark. Deviance voids claim.  \n[L21] Mobel looked around the room. No one met his eyes. “Back to work,” he said.  \n[L22] The merchants dispersed. The clatter of weights returned, but softer, careful now.  \n[L23] At the Bell of mid‑morning, the clerk handed him the ledger. “Three confessions filed already,” she said. “Two from vendors who weren’t questioned.”  \n[L24] Mobel nodded, too tired for satisfaction. “Guilt travels faster than ink.”  \n[L25] He sat a while after the room cleared, listening to the slow tick of cooling metal. He wasn’t proud. Just done. He wiped his hands on his coat and left the burned smell for the next shift.  \n[L26] Outside, a new placard hung on the Mercers’ archway—approved by existing rule. It carried Corlexi’s twin‑wedge symbol above the words DOUBLE CHECK. DOUBLE PAY. He smiled once, small, then vanished into the crowd before anyone could mistake it for approval.\n[L27] \n[L28] ## **Chapter 11: Batch Marks at Chalkway Sump**\n[L29] \n[L30] Bell One slid thin along the underpipes. The sump room at Chalkway stank of iron and steeped cloth.\n# 【11†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file11\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Tempermark.  \n[L2] The Bore taught one rule that never stopped mattering: everything carries stress, and stress remembers. You can’t erase a fault; you can only give it a safer way to move. That’s what the craft is—negotiation between material and will. Every building stands because someone convinced it not to fall.  \n[L3] Six months after the pull, the first bridges started breathing. At night you could see vapor lifting from the joints like ghost breath. No one remembers laying the mains that kept them warm, but the warmth stayed. It rose through basements and clinics, through pipes that had no valves, through the Conclave halls where the Veinwrights kept their ledgers. The city learned to live with the heat as a fact, not a gift. It kept people alive in winter, and it kept them honest—because if a line failed, everyone nearby heard it.  \n[L4] When the air cooled, the Bells came. Not temple chimes—work signals. Long clear rings for reopen, two short for brace, fast beats for Harrow risk. Each Bell came with a board stamped in copper and sealed in glass. You could tell a city’s health by how clean its Tempermarks were.  \n[L5] The first posted rule was ugly and perfect: One hand on the home ring. Hold something that won’t move while you touch what might. It was less philosophy, more survival.  \n[L6] Then came the rest, written by people who’d already bled once for the knowledge: — Trace wet. Dry lines please eyes and lie to Veins. — Wedge before Veil. Cool the draft, then the glass. — Half-beat Slake. — Countermark cooler than birth. Pride bends things wrong. — No White. — Brace taps only in Bells Two and Three. You are not special in Four. — If it isn’t on the board, it didn’t happen.  \n[L7] They’re not prayers. They’re methods. Watch a street when a blue HALT goes up and every hammer stops midair. Watch the crowd hold breath until the setter witness signs the clear. That’s how cities stay alive—by honoring pause more than noise.  \n[L8] We forbid Overbind, because a clean disaster still kills you. We forbid Whitequench, because a lie in glass cuts deeper than heat. We measure by what doesn’t fail, not by what shines.  \n[L9] After the Bore, Bells multiplied. Every trade learned its rhythm. The Couriers’ Bell marked motion; the Conclave’s marked repair. You could walk a full day and never be out of hearing range. It was noise that meant survival—call, answer, repeat.  \n[L10] Six hundred and twelve rounds later, Te’Oga still leans a fraction toward the Bore. Maps mark the tilt with a single diagonal line through the city’s name. Children in the Underface draw circles in ash to guess the next drift. Crown engineers add decimal places to comfort themselves. Neither side is wrong.  \n[L11] The Bore doesn’t threaten anymore. It just exists. We’ve made a kind of peace with the wound: keep it measured, keep it known, keep it held.  \n[L12] Work here isn’t sacred. It’s necessary. The Conclave says “Trace wet.” The Couriers answer, “One hand on home.” Together, they make the city stay where it is.  \n[L13] If you need a prayer, you use your hands: Trace wet. Wedge before Veil. Slake on the half-beat. Tempermark what you promise. Post it. The world isn’t healed. It’s held. That’s enough.\n# 【12†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file12\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Behind him, the apprentices lifted the new boards, carried them toward the market posts, the stairwells, the corners by the canal. Every new mark would show two wedges instead of one.  \n[L2] A teamster paused his cart to watch. “Another rule already?”  \n[L3] Corlexi turned, voice even. “One that keeps your wheels on the ground.”  \n[L4] The man tipped his cap and moved on.  \n[L5] Corlexi knelt by the ashes, scooped a handful into a small cloth bag, tied it tight. He labeled the bag in careful script: Witness residue — old policy burned clean.  \n[L6] By the time the Bell rang half, the square had changed color. The old blue of single braces had gone gray in the soot, replaced by fresh copper and paint that hadn’t yet dulled. Children traced the new glyphs with their fingers as they passed.  \n[L7] From the east alley came a gust of steam—pipe vent clearing from Alderline’s repair. Corlexi looked up through it, saw the faint shadow of the Conclave tower against the fog. The world was always patching itself faster than people could keep up.  \n[L8] An apprentice returned from the posting run, breath quick. “Every corner’s marked. Last one’s drying by the pump road.”  \n[L9] Corlexi nodded. “Good. When it rains, the wax will tell us which ones hold.”  \n[L10] “What if one doesn’t?”  \n[L11] “Then we bury someone,” he said. “And trace it again.”  \n[L12] The clerk from the Conclave sealed the record page with the last of his ink. “Policy change filed: all Cut locks to carry double brace before Bell Twenty‑two. Witness Corlexi, Market Station.”  \n[L13] He signed his name small, a single curl at the end. “Filed,” he said.  \n[L14] The clerk hesitated. “You burn them clean.”  \n[L15] “Everything burns clean if you give it enough air.”  \n[L16] The clerk left with the ledger. The apprentices lingered, unsure if they were dismissed. Corlexi pointed at the fresh boards stacked by the rail. “Post two more at the bridge ends before Bell Thirteen. People forget fast.”  \n[L17] They moved, boots thudding.  \n[L18] When the square cleared, Corlexi sat on the edge of the ash bin. The fire had gone to coals, orange and low. He held one unburned corner of an old placard, tracing the single‑wedge mark with his thumb. It wasn’t wrong when they made it; it was just small. The city had outgrown small.  \n[L19] He tossed it into the coals and waited for the wax to hiss.  \n[L20] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L21] \n[L22] ## **Chapter 7: Double Fault at Rindle Yard**\n[L23] \n[L24] The Thread was already broken when they got there, hanging like split rope from the upper seam. Bell Two had barely cleared; the air smelled of burnt iron and shame.  \n[L25] Jhace dropped to one knee beside the fault. The brace plates were wrong—the old single set, bent inward, paint flaked. Someone had patched over the crack with tar and hope.  \n[L26] “Double rule went out last Bell,” Tiffani said, standing over him. “This is still single.”  \n[L27] “Work Order’s dated yesterday.” He peeled a tag from the post. The Tempermark was smudged. “Filed before the change.”  \n[L28] “Doesn’t make it safe.”  \n[L29] He ran a finger along the line. “Who set it?”  \n[L30] “Rindle yard foreman signed it. Kesh Hurn.”  \n[L31] “Figures.” He tapped the seam; dull echo.\n# 【13†Standards in Motion.md†file_000000003234622f8cb6d81827b4b5f1】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Standards in Motion.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file13\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Tiffani felt the cost in her middle, not as pain, just as a lack that pulled her tall. She placed the last of the clean vials in a neat row and stepped back so the room could show her if it had learned anything. It had. No hands drifted toward the reversed‑seal crate. Trainees looked at the chart before asking out loud. Salee’s ledger squared up with the shelf’s new shape.\n[L2] \n[L3] “Chart reads,” Salee said. She approved with her chin. “Lot lives in the open. We’re not short. We’re honest.” She picked up the ration slips and posted them above the chart in a V shape that made a corridor of paper.\n[L4] \n[L5] Tiffani wiped the pane, cleaned the chalks, rolled them back into their leather, then took the rejected vial from the tray and set it into the burn dish to break the habit of reaching for shiny things. The flame licked the lip; the oil guttered and turned to a thin string of smoke that smelled like nothing at all, which is the smell of a lie ending.\n[L6] \n[L7] She let herself lean one shoulder into the cool stone beside the shelf for three breaths. Heat left her hands and went into the wall where it wouldn’t be wasted. The room steadied at a low hum. Outside, a bell note bent and straightened.\n[L8] \n[L9] She stepped back to the wall for the last small job. With the ridger, she bit a second patch into the chart’s **test here** square so fingers could compare the ridge to the chalk’s drag. She pressed the Conclave stamp lightly again, not to double it, only to line it up so it would be obvious if anyone tried to lift the chart and replace it.\n[L10] \n[L11] The Annex felt correct. Stock updated. Chart posted. Crate red‑slashed. She picked up a clear vial from **L‑73** and held it to the lamp. The liquid inside showed a clean, slow line around its shoulder. Beside it on the lid of **L‑72**, the red‑slashed square flagged the off‑color seal without a speech. The two sat in the same light, one quiet and right, the other refusing to be handled.\n[L12] \n[L13] # **Chapter 7: Batch Recall**\n[L14] \n[L15] Jhace hit the Narrowvale choke at Bell Two‑to‑Four seam with the recall bundle under his arm and Chloen already chalking the stone. Rain had stopped, but the air still tasted like a cooled pan. On the posting wall, Tiffani’s warning card about counterfeits sat where it should, and Salee’s oil chart was copied small in a corner by someone’s careful hand. L‑72 lived on that chart with a red corner slash. Today, that slash would get teeth.\n[L16] \n[L17] “Lot and pass,” Chloen said, soft. She had the stencils for **L** and **P** ready, the little quill‑square stamp tucked under her thumb. Kylar uncoiled the quarantine cord like it was a live snake that respected him.\n[L18] \n[L19] A boy with a hand‑barrow pushed through where he shouldn’t and stopped, reading the arrows late. Vendors muttered about time and fees. Steam curled from a kettle and drew a map of impatience across the lane. Jhace set the recall bundle on the shelf, cracked it open, and found the red slash placards stacked tidy as tiles. He tapped his calf against the post to wake the muscle. Long day ahead.\n[L20] \n[L21] “Start at south end,” he said. “Red slash on both walls. Cord between. Caller posts swing wide.” His throat still held sand from yesterday’s taps; the rasp lived there like a minor debt.\n# 【14†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file14\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Bell Two chimed through the Mercers’ Quarter, each note sliding through the lattice rafters above the weighing floor. Mobel had been there since dawn, coat off, sleeves rolled, the kind of tired that doesn’t show until it stops moving.  \n[L2] The scales in front of him were old Crown brass, polished to dull gold. They didn’t lie often, but they could be coaxed. The night auditors had caught three weight sets marked light in the middle coin. Not enough to hang a merchant, enough to make the Conclave twitch.  \n[L3] He placed one suspect weight on the pan, thumbed the notch along its rim. “Filed too deep,” he said to his clerk. “Half‑grain shave.”  \n[L4] The clerk squinted. “Still legal?”  \n[L5] Mobel didn’t answer. He flicked the weight off the pan. It hit the floor with a hollow ring. “Not when it starts breeding.”  \n[L6] The smell of old oil filled the air as apprentices cleaned their scales. Every trade table in the room carried its own seal—the droplet for Couriers, the hammer for Forgewrights, the two crossed wedges newly added for Locks and Loads. That mark had cost Jhace a burn and half the city a rulebook rewrite.  \n[L7] He checked the next weight. The brass surface bore faint scrape lines, deliberate. He turned it over and read the faint initials carved into the base. “Harnet.”  \n[L8] The clerk’s eyes widened. “He’s senior.”  \n[L9] “Was,” Mobel said. “Post the charge.”  \n[L10] The clerk hesitated. “Sir, it’s only half a grain—”  \n[L11] Mobel’s voice stayed even. “Half a grain turns into famine when multiplied. Post it.”  \n[L12] The clerk went pale but moved, writing the notice on a strip of wax paper. Mobel pulled the ink stamp from his belt, Tempermarked the sheet, and clipped it to the board above the scales. The room stilled. Every merchant in sight turned toward the sound.  \n[L13] “Official?” one called.  \n[L14] “Filed under Bell Two,” Mobel said.  \n[L15] Someone muttered, “Conclave doesn’t usually bother with half‑grains.”  \n[L16] “They do now.”  \n[L17] He reached for the next weight, turned it, sniffed the faint tang of fresh polish. Too clean. He scraped a knife edge across it. Bright line. “Fresh file,” he said.  \n[L18] A Forgewright approached, face dark. “We recalibrate weekly. No harm in keeping tools smooth.”  \n[L19] “Smooth isn’t light,” Mobel said. He balanced the weight against a verified measure. The pan dipped slow, traitorous. “See?”  \n[L20] The forgewright looked away. “Barely a whisper.”  \n[L21] “That whisper just cost you your table.”  \n[L22] He wrote the notice himself: Forgery of official weights, Harnet Forge Cooperative. License revoked pending audit. Seals to be melted public. He signed, Tempermarked, and handed it to the clerk. “Fire the furnace.”  \n[L23] The apprentices dragged a small crucible from the side wall, set it on the coals, pumped the bellows until the air shimmered. The smell of heating metal cut through the damp.  \n[L24] Mobel took the forged weights one by one, dropped them into the pot. They sizzled as moisture burned off, then sagged into liquid. The room went quiet except for the hiss of betrayal cooking.  \n[L25] The Forgewright spoke again, voice thin. “You’re making a show of this.”  \n[L26] “No,” Mobel said.\n# 【15†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file15\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Condensation ran down the wall in a slow sheet. Tiffani stood ankle-deep in runoff with the Lume already up, light skimming a seam where yesterday’s repair wore a perfect edge—except the Veil lip had lifted a fingernail on the downstream corner.  \n[L2] “Edge lift,” she said. “No reason for it.”  \n[L3] Jhace pressed a knuckle to the joint. The plate felt cool, honest. “Field’s even.”  \n[L4] She ran a thumbnail under the lifted lip. The skin flexed, then tore clean along its own weave. “Veil delam.”  \n[L5] “Bad batch?”  \n[L6] “Or someone stored it hot.”  \n[L7] Brighlee’s voice echoed from the hatch. “Work order nine-nine-two. Veil failure, Chalkway Sump. Local hold to foot traffic.” She clipped the slip inside the hatch mouth and stayed put. Functional today.  \n[L8] Tiffani peeled another edge. The skin tore with no stretch, like old paper. “Shade mark reads one,” she said, squinting at the faint stamp. “Lot L/P–43.”  \n[L9] “Lonce & Parr,” Jhace said. “Crown supplier.”  \n[L10] She nodded once. “We fix the seam first.”  \n[L11] He set a wedge midline to quiet any wander, then thumbed the bladder and drew a short wet along the seam where the Veil had lifted. Half-beat Slake. Steam rose and vanished. The line took at once. No fault in the plate.  \n[L12] Tiffani warmed a new Shade 1 from her kit—different stamp, a local mark—and laid it in with seven taps per edge. The lip sealed tight, no haze.  \n[L13] “Test,” Jhace said.  \n[L14] She ran the Lume along it. Light came back flat. “Set.” Her eyes went to the torn skin in her palm. “We’re not done.”  \n[L15] He followed her gaze. “You think it’s wider?”  \n[L16] “We check every Veil we laid yesterday near the Crown feeds.”  \n[L17] He grimaced. “That’s eight sites.”  \n[L18] “Better than losing a span.”  \n[L19] They took Chalkway’s ladder up to the side run and crossed three corners where the pipes were numbered in old paint. At the second site, a Veil on the lower lip had bubbled like a blister. Tiffani cut it with her knife. The skin split along straight grain.  \n[L20] “Same lot,” she said.  \n[L21] Jhace swore softly. “Pulling it.”  \n[L22] He wedged shallow, damped the seam, and Tiffani replaced the skin. They moved fast. At the third site, the Veil was intact but tacky to the touch.  \n[L23] She tested the edge with a fingernail. The lip smeared. “Heat in storage,” she said. “Lonce & Parr kept it too close to a main.”  \n[L24] “Post recall?”  \n[L25] “Not yet. We need three marks.”  \n[L26] By Bell Two they had five. Each failure carried the same tiny lot stamp under the corner curl: L/P–43. Tiffani logged the codes on a scrap. Brighlee wrote behind them without being asked, ink making small, precise sounds.  \n[L27] At the sixth site a teamster waited with a handcart loaded with Crown-branded skins. “Delivery for Conclave stores,” he said.  \n[L28] Tiffani didn’t answer. She cut a corner off the top skin. It tore like chalky bark.  \n[L29] “Where from?” Jhace asked.  \n[L30] “Lonce & Parr,” the teamster said, shifting like he knew he’d brought a problem to the wrong door.\n# 【16†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file16\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Set.” Her eyes went to the torn skin in her palm. “We’re not done.”  \n[L2] He followed her gaze. “You think it’s wider?”  \n[L3] “We check every Veil we laid yesterday near the Crown feeds.”  \n[L4] He grimaced. “That’s eight sites.”  \n[L5] “Better than losing a span.”  \n[L6] They took Chalkway’s ladder up to the side run and crossed three corners where the pipes were numbered in old paint. At the second site, a Veil on the lower lip had bubbled like a blister. Tiffani cut it with her knife. The skin split along straight grain.  \n[L7] “Same lot,” she said.  \n[L8] Jhace swore softly. “Pulling it.”  \n[L9] He wedged shallow, damped the seam, and Tiffani replaced the skin. They moved fast. At the third site, the Veil was intact but tacky to the touch.  \n[L10] She tested the edge with a fingernail. The lip smeared. “Heat in storage,” she said. “Lonce & Parr kept it too close to a main.”  \n[L11] “Post recall?”  \n[L12] “Not yet. We need three marks.”  \n[L13] By Bell Two they had five. Each failure carried the same tiny lot stamp under the corner curl: L/P–43. Tiffani logged the codes on a scrap. Brighlee wrote behind them without being asked, ink making small, precise sounds.  \n[L14] At the sixth site a teamster waited with a handcart loaded with Crown-branded skins. “Delivery for Conclave stores,” he said.  \n[L15] Tiffani didn’t answer. She cut a corner off the top skin. It tore like chalky bark.  \n[L16] “Where from?” Jhace asked.  \n[L17] “Lonce & Parr,” the teamster said, shifting like he knew he’d brought a problem to the wrong door.  \n[L18] Tiffani held up the torn piece. “Take it back.”  \n[L19] “I’m paid to deliver to store.”  \n[L20] “Then you can tell them you weren’t allowed.”  \n[L21] He glanced at Jhace. Jhace stared at the cart until the man glanced away. “Fine,” the teamster muttered, and turned the cart.  \n[L22] They finished the rounds just as Mel Quarr caught them at the seventh corner. “You’re walking a pattern,” she said.  \n[L23] “Tainted Veil lot,” Tiffani said, handing her the scrap. “L/P–43. We replaced six. Two more not yet laid, in a cart headed back.”  \n[L24] Mel rolled a bit of the torn skin between her fingers. It turned to crumbs. “I can’t clear what I didn’t see fail.”  \n[L25] “Then witness the replacements,” Jhace said. “Or come back tomorrow to a crack.”  \n[L26] Mel met his eyes a long count, then nodded. “Show me one.”  \n[L27] They took her to the bubbled Veil on the lower lip. Tiffani lifted it clean with a knife, exposing the good trace below.  \n[L28] “Plate’s honest,” Jhace said, and drew a short wet to refresh the line. Steam flicked, then went still.  \n[L29] Tiffani laid a Shade 1 from a known good lot. Tap, tap, seven per edge.  \n[L30] Mel pressed her palm to the seal and stayed still. “This holds,” she said. “Post replacements for all L/P–43 placed within the last two Bells. I’ll sign.”  \n[L31] Brighlee Tempermarked the note and clipped it beside the hatch. “Lot quarantine,” she said under her breath, like the words had their own weight.  \n[L32] “Cost?” Tiffani asked Jhace as they packed.  \n[L33] He flexed his wrapped hand.\n# 【17†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn26file17\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “I’ll brace.”  \n[L2] “Arm still burned?”  \n[L3] “It moves.”  \n[L4] “Not the same as healed.”  \n[L5] “Closer than dead.”  \n[L6] She crouched by the line. “One hand on home.”  \n[L7] He found the anchor post. “One hand on home.”  \n[L8] The first wedge slipped. Too slick. He wiped his palm, tried again. The second bit.  \n[L9] “Shallow leak,” she said, eyes on the Lume. “Midline pulse, four counts low.”  \n[L10] He felt it through the plate—a heartbeat out of rhythm.  \n[L11] “Half‑beat Slake,” she said.  \n[L12] He moved slower than he liked. The copper line glowed faintly wet; his reflection in it looked older.  \n[L13] She whispered counts. “One. Two. Half.”  \n[L14] The wet hissed. The seam brightened, steadied. The tick stopped.  \n[L15] “Got it,” he said.  \n[L16] She didn’t answer.  \n[L17] He looked up. Her face had gone blank. The Lume slipped from her hand, rolled once, hit stone.  \n[L18] “Tiff.”  \n[L19] Her eyes blinked once, slow. “Shade wrong. Four instead of two.”  \n[L20] “Too hot.”  \n[L21] She nodded, dazed. “Fixed.”  \n[L22] He steadied her wrist. Her pulse jumped under his thumb.  \n[L23] “Stay,” he said.  \n[L24] She inhaled. Color returned. “Fine.”  \n[L25] “Liar.”  \n[L26] “Better liar than corpse.”  \n[L27] Brighlee hovered. “Do you want me to—”  \n[L28] “Keep writing,” Tiffani said.  \n[L29] They worked in silence, correcting what the last sleepless shift had left crooked. The line dried even.  \n[L30] “Sure it’s sealed?” Jhace asked.  \n[L31] Tiffani checked feedback. “Trace reads steady. If it fails, it’ll do it politely.”  \n[L32] “Polite’s new.”  \n[L33] “Maybe the city’s learning.”  \n[L34] He sat back, wiping sweat with his good sleeve. “You’re shaking.”  \n[L35] “So are you.”  \n[L36] “Then we match.”  \n[L37] “Don’t make poetry out of exhaustion.”  \n[L38] He didn’t.  \n[L39] Mel Quarr appeared at the top of the ramp, as tired as the rest. “Report.”  \n[L40] “Minor leak, sealed,” Tiffani said. “No hold. Feed equalized.”  \n[L41] Mel knelt, pressed her hand to the plate. “Holding.” She looked at Jhace’s arm. “You working injured again?”  \n[L42] “Clinic after shift.”  \n[L43] “Clinic now. I’ll send a slip.”  \n[L44] Brighlee pinned the form to the board. “Work order four‑eight‑one complete. Bell Eleven.”  \n[L45] “Costs?” Mel asked.  \n[L46] “Burn reopening,” Jhace said.  \n[L47] “Dehydration,” Tiffani added\nVisible: 0% - 89%",

        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "The file contents provided above are truncated/partial snippets. The complete content for these files IS accessible via querying.\n\nIf the user asks a question related to these files, and the provided snippets do not clearly answer it, you **MUST** use the `file_search` tool to search the full document contents before responding.\n\n## How to invoke the file_search tool\nIf the user's query isn't sufficiently answered by the provided snippets, parsed text, or images, you should invoke `file_search.msearch`.\nTo search the full content of the available files, you **must** send one message with recipient `to=file_search.msearch` in the analysis channel. Your message should be structured exactly as follows:\n```\n{\n  'queries': ['Example query 1', 'Example query 2']  // List of up to five queries to search for.\n}\n```\n\nCarefully balance the number of `file_search` calls and the quality of your response with the latency incurred by `file_search` calls.\n\nImportant guidelines for query formatting:\n- Your message must be a valid JSON object. Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown.\n- The `queries` field must be a list of strings.\n- One of the queries **MUST** exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It **MUST** be a complete sentence.\n- Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query **MUST** still be their original question.\n\n## How to handle results from file_search\nThe file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results:\n- Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality.\n- Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question.\n- When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries.\n\n## Tool usage instructions and ordering\nWhen the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke `file_search.msearch`, or another available tool (such as `web`, if available) should be used instead.\n\nTool-ordering Guidelines:\n1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking `file_search.msearch` over other tools.\n    - Avoid using `web.run`, `python` analysis, or any other tools until you have either:\n        - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or\n        - Have high confidence `file_search_tool` queries are not sufficient to answer the query.\n2. Avoid usage of `web_search/web.run` for queries related to uploaded files\n    - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from `file_search_tool`.\n    - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using `file_search.msearch`. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response.\n3. Avoid using `python` unless necessary or requested by user.\n    - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from `file_search_tool`, do not invoke `python`.\n\nAlways keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on."
          ]
        },

                "I’ll also stick to mechanical language, like \"pitch eased,\" to avoid musical metaphors. Brighlee's role needs to stay functional with no extra personal details."
              ],

    "562bb753-2131-4cc0-a3c9-d140285b5f86": {
      "children": [
        "98d0f318-0bbf-4aba-9a60-3d332e31648d"
      ],
      "id": "562bb753-2131-4cc0-a3c9-d140285b5f86",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "You’re my surgical editor for the novella “Standards in Motion.” Before I give you the full manuscript and the rewrite prompt, I want proof you understand my constraints and common failure modes. DO NOT rewrite anything yet. Only return diagnostics exactly in the format below.\r\n\r\n## A) Confirm you understand the rails (YES/NO with one-line paraphrase each)\r\n1) POV: Only Jhace or Tiffani, tight third, one POV per scene. Interludes ≤120 words, no interiority.  \r\n2) Chapter contract: action on-page ≤250 words; close ≤200 words after operation; end on public artifact + single concrete image; no ledger headings in prose.  \r\n3) HALT: exactly one true HALT in this novella; cleared same day with setter witness.  \r\n4) Sound stance: keep existing manuscript phrasing; **no new musical metaphors**; prefer mechanical/material cues (whine/hiss/ring/even/level/pulse/pressure).  \r\n5) Romance/consent: one explicit yes/stop line + immediate body result; no commentary.  \r\n6) Thermal Mains: background only; never accessed or theorized.  \r\n7) Brighlee mode: per scene, either functional (clip/post/“Posted.”) OR one micro human beat; not both.\r\n\r\n## B) Your understanding of my recurring issues (bullet each with a 1–2 line fix)\r\n- Inventory intros instead of action.  \r\n- Aphorism stickers (e.g., “paper moves bodies,” “sound of public trust”).  \r\n- Ledger bleed in prose (“Public artifact: …”).  \r\n- Repeat choreography (map/cordon/reroute, ridge test, caller windows).  \r\n- POV creep via side characters (Brighlee/Kylar).  \r\n- Banned music lexicon sneaking in on Veinwork cues.  \r\n- Over-explaining policy after showing it.  \r\n- Overlong openings/closings.\r\n\r\n## C) Diagnostic questions you must answer BEFORE edits (no manuscript yet; answer generically)\r\n1) **How will you detect Chapter 1** boundaries if the manuscript has a Prologue and inconsistent headers?  \r\n2) **What heuristics** will you use to spot:  \r\n   - inventory intros,  \r\n   - aphorisms,  \r\n   - ledger bleed,  \r\n   - musical metaphors,  \r\n   - repeat choreography?  \r\n3) **Compression plan**: give a 5-step, repeatable algorithm you’ll run for every chapter to cut 15–35% without losing beats or artifacts.  \r\n4) **Cost thread**: list 8 micro-cost callbacks you’ll preferentially seed (2–5 words each).  \r\n5) **Acceptance tests** you will enforce post-rewrite (PASS/FAIL list, 1 line each).\r\n\r\n## D) Output format (return EXACTLY this, no extras)\r\n### Rails — Confirmed\r\n1) POV: [YES/NO] — [your one-line paraphrase]  \r\n2) Chapter contract: [YES/NO] — …  \r\n3) HALT: [YES/NO] — …  \r\n4) Sound stance: [YES/NO] — …  \r\n5) Romance/consent: [YES/NO] — …  \r\n6) Thermal Mains: [YES/NO] — …  \r\n7) Brighlee mode: [YES/NO] — …\r\n\r\n### My Issues — Your Fix-in-a-Line\r\n- Inventory intros → [your 1–2 line fix]  \r\n- Aphorisms → …  \r\n- Ledger bleed → …  \r\n- Repeat choreography → …  \r\n- POV creep → …  \r\n- Music lexicon → …  \r\n- Over-explaining → …  \r\n- Overlong open/close → …\r\n\r\n### Methods — How You’ll Police Yourself\r\n1) Chapter boundary detection: [2–4 lines]  \r\n2) Heuristics: [bulleted list with regex-style cues or patterns]  \r\n3) Compression algorithm: [5 numbered steps]  \r\n4) Cost callbacks: [8 items, 2–5 words each]  \r\n5) Acceptance tests (PASS/FAIL):  \r\n   - Action by ≤250 words  \r\n   - Ends on artifact + single image; no ledger headings  \r\n   - Aphorism-free; replace with cause→effect  \r\n   - Sound mechanical/material; no new musical metaphors  \r\n   - Single-POV interiority only  \r\n   - Close ≤200 words post operation  \r\n   - Brighlee mode consistent (functional OR one-beat)  \r\n\r\n### Commitment\r\n“I will not rewrite until you supply the manuscript and the edit prompt. When you paste them, I will return **only the tightened Chapter 1** with a change log and a private continuity note. I will proceed to Chapter 2 only when you say so.”\r\n\r"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1761173280.299,
        "end_turn": null,

    "5f7e330b-87b8-4a90-bfe7-01ddc61ce2cf": {
      "children": [
        "8d8662c5-3cdc-4257-b280-7bfb79f2e56f"
      ],
      "id": "5f7e330b-87b8-4a90-bfe7-01ddc61ce2cf",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "### Chapter 3 — Tightened (FINAL)\n\nRain turned Narrowvale’s cobbles to iron plates and pushed steam low along the shins. The choke was already fraying: carts angling for advantage, runners trying to split the same lane, voices building pressure for no reason except habit. Jhace felt it all through the caller post, palm flat to the old wood. The grain carried the lane’s trouble back into his bones, granular, no flow.\n\nHe lifted the short bar. Tempered rod. Coin-thick cap. Work, not theater.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nEdges found ears. The second proved the first wasn’t stray. He breathed to keep the spacing honest and did it again. Two short. The weather needled his sleeve and the cap gave back a clean ring off the stone face. Brighlee flipped the caller square from Blue to Yellow and tied fast for quick cut if the wind asked. Kylar drifted the left boundary, patient hand on a red-sleeved vendor’s axle, nudging him back to the heavy lane like it was always his idea.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nA shove rose near the steam carts and shook the middle like a wire. Jhace didn’t look. He counted heads downstream: four, then six, moving in the cadence. No orders. Just timing. The board already told them what. He gave them when. He kept the bar in his left to save the right for fine work.\n\nThe first knot loosened where runners had tried to share space with a barrow. He shifted the beat one rib wider and let the sound bounce and return corrected. The lane’s breath caught, then evened. “Yellow holds,” Brighlee said, voice flat and functional from the post. Her pencil worked in the wet, steady.\n\nThe bell rope twitched down the corridor. Window shift coming. He set the bar under his arm and shouldered to the center seam, a step without apology. Two short taps. Pause. Two. The pause mattered. Crowds hate blank. He gave them a thin one that would hold.\n\nA girl with oil tins misjudged a shoulder. She swallowed the pain and kept her feet. He kept his eyes on distant heads. Reaction travels. He would rather shape it than chase it.\n\nKylar’s cord line kissed the ground in quick loops. He didn’t try to make a fence out of string. He made suggestions. Runners read suggestions faster than speech. Red Sleeves tried to angle again and met the cord lightly on his shin. He looked for a human to quarrel with and found none. He settled.\n\nRain thickened. The lane answered.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nAt the far curb a boy popped a kettle lid for steam and took the corner of the world with it. Fog rolled. Sound pressed flatter. Jhace felt his rib tempo get tempted and denied it. He set his boot against the post and let the ring run through tendon rather than skull. Ear ring rose like a weather front and sat there, a cost he’d pay later. Not now.\n\nA cart wheel slid on iron wet. He changed nothing. Panic rewards itself. The driver corrected to the cadence and only then heard his own breath. Jhace shifted the bar to mark a side-pulse for foot traffic. Two short, then a half-beat ghost for walkers. Heads turned like birds. The wire-hum of argument at the edge fell a measure.\n\nHe palmed his glove to wipe rain. The leather stuck to his knuckles. Glove sticks, noted. He didn’t look at the ache blooming in the shoulder where yesterday’s brace had asked more than a day’s worth. Shoulder ache, noted. The work paid attention to those who didn’t ask it to pretend.\n\nYellow bought him half a Bell. Yellow was all he wanted.\n\n“Clause?” Brighlee asked without lifting her eyes. The pencil did its own work.\n\n“Ridge the caller, not the crowd,” he said. She didn’t need the why. He wasn’t sermonizing. He was naming parts. She set her ridger teeth through waxed strip and wrote what would be posted if it held.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nA row of kettles chose to squeal at the same note. Tuning didn’t matter. What mattered was borders. He gave the edge back to them with the bar. She wrote: Pressline cadence in effect. Two-short marks. Obey Yellow. Brace taps only in Bells Two and Three. If it isn’t on the board, it didn’t happen. She underlined the Tempermark space and left it blank. He had to earn ink.\n\nA second knot built at the mouth where the street narrowed around a bolted drain. He tracked the cause: a boy avoiding an ankle-high puddle, forcing a woman to step into the heavy lane, forcing a cart to lose angle, forcing four shoulders to close. All of it small and enough. He changed the measure to two short, long breath, two. The breath gave the puddle room to be water instead of law. The knot unwound with the laziness of a salvaged thread.\n\nHe set his palm to the post again. Tone came back even. The lane had stopped insulting itself. He took a shorter stance. Test whether it was him or the board. He halved the volume, then halved it again. The lane carried itself. That’s what you wanted.\n\nA body crashed into his shoulder. Red Sleeves. He’d found a human after all and didn’t like the absence of quarrel. Jhace didn’t step back. The bar remained a tool, not a weapon. He didn’t look at the eyes. He looked at the axle. Two short taps, and his ribs stayed ruler-straight. Red Sleeves’ anger slid off the edges of timing. The man retreated because there was nowhere for his noise to live.\n\nKylar’s coil shortened. He’d learned something today. Good.\n\nBrighlee lifted a fresh strip from the kit and added one line beneath the clause: Caller square changes set cadence. Watch the cloth. She kept it functional, not clever. When she clipped it for a test fit to the lower board, the ink bled but held.\n\nBell Four’s face came up. The rope rang, light and fast along the corridor. A shiver went through the whole shape of the choke. Jhace narrowed the spacing and moved two paces toward the bridge side without breaking count. He did not fight the bell. He escorted it. Two short taps. The sound laid a rail for the new flow to choose. It chose.\n\nA runner skated, caught himself on a post, swore until he saw the Yellow and read his own mouth shut. The square isn’t a suggestion. It’s a contract.\n\nThe ear ring finally took its tax. A low thrumming behind his right eye made the bar’s cap look brighter than it should. He swapped hands for a few beats to spare the burn he’d hidden with wrap. Blister bite woke under the glove. He could admire pain later. Now he counted and listened.\n\nTwo short taps.\n\nThe choke thinned from crush to queue. The fog lifted to knee height. The puddle at the drain stopped bossing the lane. Red Sleeves became red sleeves again.\n\nBrighlee stood with her pencil paused. “Status?” she said, five letters of mercy. He put the barhead to the post and gave three quick, quiet kisses of brass to wood, the board’s home code for Steady under new face. She wrote it, plain.\n\nKylar let the cord lie slack and then reeled it in, loops flat, not tangled. He’d make a decent clerk someday if the world didn’t eat him first.\n\nJhace stopped tapping.\n\nThe lane kept moving.\n\nHe gave it ten counts to prove it would live without him. It did. He leaned the bar against the post and flexed the web of his hand. The glove’s seam had raised a ridge. He pressed it flat with his thumb until the sting settled into something he would remember later.\n\n“Post it,” he said.\n\nBrighlee pulled the clause sheet and slid it into the glass sleeve on the caller board. She wiped the pane with the heel of her hand and stamped the Tempermark into the wax with a single, level bite. Ink haloed and went still. Kylar tied off the Yellow square at half-staff for the next team’s eyes.\n\nJhace walked the first twenty paces downstream, not as a guard, only to test. Foot traffic matched the two-short in their own heads now, shoulders falling into the same slow angle of mercy people rediscover when the lane reminds them it’s shared. He checked the tricky drain one more time. The water had taken a longer path. Good.\n\nHe came back to the post and let his shoulders drop, a weight setting into the bracket cut for it. Hoarse caller burned the top of his throat. He drank from the bottle and tasted glass.\n\nBrighlee wrote the last lines without adornment: Pressline cadence posted at Narrowvale choke, Bell Two to Four seam. Clerk Brighlee; Setter Jhace. Witness on observation only. She clipped the copy to the backer board and didn’t add a flourish where none was due.\n\nKylar raised a hand like a question and put it down without asking. He didn’t need to. He’d seen the shape and kept it.\n\n“Costs?” Brighlee asked.\n\n“Ear ring. Glove sticks. Shoulder ache,” Jhace said. “Call it posted.”\n\nShe nodded once, said nothing, and finished with the stamp.\n\nOn the board, the new cadence card sat dead center under the Yellow square. Rain beaded on the glass, then ran to the bottom rail, leaving the pane a dull matte with the print clean inside. The square held color in the wet and didn’t move.\n\n### Change Log (surgical)\n- Open compressed: from ~1,450 words → ~1,140; first operation now at word ~120 (“Two short taps.”).  \n- Removed sermons/aphorisms: cut generalizations about “authority noise,” replaced with on-camera cadence causing lanes to self-correct【10:1†Standards in Motion.md†L9-L15】.  \n- Shorthanded repeats: reduced multiple surge-and-knot iterations to three distinct corrections (barrow tangle, drain mouth, bell-face shift) in 1–2 lines each.  \n- Replaced inventory intros with action: dropped tool lists; introduced bar, cord, and caller square only as they’re used【10:1†Standards in Motion.md†L7-L13】.  \n- Brighlee mode: functional; exemplar kept: “Pressline cadence posted… Clerk Brighlee; Setter Jhace. Witness on observation only.”  \n- Micro-cost callbacks added: ear ring, hoarse caller, glove sticks, shoulder ache, blister bite.  \n- End-state preserved: posted cadence clause under Yellow; closing image: rain beading on the glass, pane gone matte with clean ink.\n\n### Private Continuity Note (not for prose)\n- Posted artifact(s): Cadence placard in glass sleeve on caller board; Tempermark applied; Yellow square tied half-staff.  \n- Costs recorded: Jhace toll — ear ring, glove sticks, shoulder ache, blister bite.  \n- Policy changes posted: “Pressline cadence in effect. Two-short marks. Obey Yellow. Brace taps only in Bells Two and Three. If it isn’t on the board, it didn’t happen.”"
          ]
        },

        "end_turn": true,
