This one holds.”  \n[L7] “Thanks,” Tiffani said.  \n[L8] The crew packed down. Jhace wiped the wedges clean, brushed stone dust off the lip, checked the band for tightness. The arch held still.  \n[L9] Below, the carts started again. A teamster looked up once, tipped his chin, and moved on. The bridge carried weight like it meant to.  \n[L10] They left the scaffold, walking down into the tunnel of cooled air beneath the arch. The smell of stone after steam clung to them both.  \n[L11] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L12] \n[L13] ## **Chapter 4: Heat Splice under Alderline**\n[L14] \n[L15] Bell Eight hit in dull echoes under Alderline’s underbelly, the sound rolling slow through stone and steam. It was late afternoon, gray and thick, the kind of day that made metal taste of air instead of earth. The lower conduits had been running hot for two Bells past limit—enough to burn shade off a wall skin. By the time Jhace reached the site, sweat had already stripped the chalk from his collar.  \n[L16] Tiffani stood by the south vent where the duct wall buckled. Her veil cloth was half soaked, but her voice stayed level. “Thermal vein tore through the splice. You feel the air?”  \n[L17] He crouched, palm to conduit. The heat bit through the glove fast. “Alive and angry. Vein’s running blind.”  \n[L18] “Blind means fast,” she said. “And if it finds the main, we’ll have a blow under the market.”  \n[L19] “Field direction?”  \n[L20] “East to west, downhill. No counter. Just run.”  \n[L21] Brighlee appeared, collar up against the heat. “Work order one-one-five-four. Heat Splice under Alderline. Setter Jhace brast, Glass Tiffani veld. Local hold to foot traffic until clear.” She Tempermarked the droplet board with shaking fingers; the wax nearly ran before it set.  \n[L22] “Chain it,” Tiffani said.  \n[L23] Jhace fastened his tether to the wall ring, one hand gripping it. “One hand on home.” The metal burned even through his sleeve. “We wedge the run first.”  \n[L24] “Use the short wedges,” she said. “The long ones will swell.”  \n[L25] He pulled two half-lengths from his pouch, jammed the first at the upper seam of the duct mouth, the second two spans below. He hammered them in until the pitch rose high and sharp.  \n[L26] The duct noise shifted—deep to shallow, like breath caught in the chest.  \n[L27] “Trace wet,” she said.  \n[L28] He thumbed the bladder cap, drew water into the copper nib, and began the run. The first line hissed as it met the heat; vapor curled into ribbons. He kept the nib steady, shoulder over hand, driving water into the split vein until the color under the metal dulled from white to gray.  \n[L29] Tiffani moved her Lume rod closer. “Shade four to six,” she said.\n# 【4†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file4\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Cost?” she asked.  \n[L2] He flexed the hand; skin pulled. “Grip’s gone for a day.”  \n[L3] “Clinic,” she said.  \n[L4] “I said a day, not a week.”  \n[L5] “Policy doesn’t care what you said.”  \n[L6] He laughed once.  \n[L7] The lock groaned, then quieted. Mel stood, brushing grit from her coat. “You held it,” she said. “Don’t expect thanks.”  \n[L8] “Wouldn’t know what to do with it,” Jhace said.  \n[L9] She almost smiled. “Good. You’ll get another call before dawn.”  \n[L10] She left up the wet stairs, boots leaving perfect prints.  \n[L11] Tiffani leaned on the gate, breathing hard. “You almost drowned.”  \n[L12] He nodded. “Almost fixed it, too.”  \n[L13] Her hand found his wrist, still wet. “Stay,” she said.  \n[L14] He did.  \n[L15] They stood until the canal calmed and the sound of the Bells faded under the water’s slow rise.\n[L16] \n[L17] ## **Chapter 6: Corlexi’s Fire (Interlude)**\n[L18] \n[L19] Bell Twelve rolled flat over the roofs, muffled by mist and smoke. Corlexi stood in the market square with both hands in the ash bin, pulling placards out one by one. Each card bore the old single‑brace symbol: two wedges facing inward, out of date by one Bell. Every lock failure that week had carried that mark. Policy changed this morning. Someone had to burn the proof.  \n[L20] He held the first card by its corner. The wax Tempermark bled blue when the heat touched it, curling into itself like muscle recoiling. The stamp read Greymantle Cut—Work Order 2043\\. He remembered the red handprint rumor before he saw the stain burned into the ink edge. “Lucky he didn’t drown,” he muttered, and dropped it into the flame.  \n[L21] A group of apprentices watched from the steps, carrying fresh boards. They whispered to each other, eyes on the burn box. “Double brace rule?” one asked.  \n[L22] Corlexi didn’t look up. “Double brace rule. Within ten rounds.”  \n[L23] The fire spat once. Wax smell thickened. The second placard carried a name he knew—Maren hilt, a good setter who’d cracked a knee when a brace failed two months ago. He held it longer before letting it fall.  \n[L24] On the far side of the square, the Couriers’ Bell office was already laying out the new boards. Ink crews worked off a folding table, stamping wedge glyphs two‑by‑two, copper heads hot enough to hiss when they touched wood. The pattern had changed overnight: old inward braces replaced by paired diagonals, more metal, more safety.  \n[L25] “Quicker this way?” an apprentice asked.  \n[L26] “No,” Corlexi said. “Slower, safer.”  \n[L27] He fed the third placard into the bin. The crowd near the food stalls had gone quiet, watching the smoke. It wasn’t ceremony; it was relief disguised as boredom.  \n[L28] A Conclave clerk crossed the square with a basket of new seals. “Posting priority corners first,” he said, voice careful. “The Conclave wants public compliance before dusk.”  \n[L29] Corlexi wiped sweat from his cheek with the back of his arm. “They always do.”  \n[L30] The clerk hesitated, holding a placard halfway. “You were there for Lowcourt, right?”  \n[L31] “Doesn’t matter where I was.” He took the placard and set it into the fire. “Now I’m here.”  \n[L32] The last of the old signs turned to smoke. He raked the ashes with a long iron rod until the glyphs broke apart.\n# 【5†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file5\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “I’ll brace.”  \n[L2] “Arm still burned?”  \n[L3] “It moves.”  \n[L4] “Not the same as healed.”  \n[L5] “Closer than dead.”  \n[L6] She crouched by the line. “One hand on home.”  \n[L7] He found the anchor post. “One hand on home.”  \n[L8] The first wedge slipped. Too slick. He wiped his palm, tried again. The second bit.  \n[L9] “Shallow leak,” she said, eyes on the Lume. “Midline pulse, four counts low.”  \n[L10] He felt it through the plate—a heartbeat out of rhythm.  \n[L11] “Half‑beat Slake,” she said.  \n[L12] He moved slower than he liked. The copper line glowed faintly wet; his reflection in it looked older.  \n[L13] She whispered counts. “One. Two. Half.”  \n[L14] The wet hissed. The seam brightened, steadied. The tick stopped.  \n[L15] “Got it,” he said.  \n[L16] She didn’t answer.  \n[L17] He looked up. Her face had gone blank. The Lume slipped from her hand, rolled once, hit stone.  \n[L18] “Tiff.”  \n[L19] Her eyes blinked once, slow. “Shade wrong. Four instead of two.”  \n[L20] “Too hot.”  \n[L21] She nodded, dazed. “Fixed.”  \n[L22] He steadied her wrist. Her pulse jumped under his thumb.  \n[L23] “Stay,” he said.  \n[L24] She inhaled. Color returned. “Fine.”  \n[L25] “Liar.”  \n[L26] “Better liar than corpse.”  \n[L27] Brighlee hovered. “Do you want me to—”  \n[L28] “Keep writing,” Tiffani said.  \n[L29] They worked in silence, correcting what the last sleepless shift had left crooked. The line dried even.  \n[L30] “Sure it’s sealed?” Jhace asked.  \n[L31] Tiffani checked feedback. “Trace reads steady. If it fails, it’ll do it politely.”  \n[L32] “Polite’s new.”  \n[L33] “Maybe the city’s learning.”  \n[L34] He sat back, wiping sweat with his good sleeve. “You’re shaking.”  \n[L35] “So are you.”  \n[L36] “Then we match.”  \n[L37] “Don’t make poetry out of exhaustion.”  \n[L38] He didn’t.  \n[L39] Mel Quarr appeared at the top of the ramp, as tired as the rest. “Report.”  \n[L40] “Minor leak, sealed,” Tiffani said. “No hold. Feed equalized.”  \n[L41] Mel knelt, pressed her hand to the plate. “Holding.” She looked at Jhace’s arm. “You working injured again?”  \n[L42] “Clinic after shift.”  \n[L43] “Clinic now. I’ll send a slip.”  \n[L44] Brighlee pinned the form to the board. “Work order four‑eight‑one complete. Bell Eleven.”  \n[L45] “Costs?” Mel asked.  \n[L46] “Burn reopening,” Jhace said.  \n[L47] “Dehydration,” Tiffani added.  \n[L48] Mel wrote both. “Clinic, mandatory.”  \n[L49] They didn’t argue.  \n[L50] When the others left, Jhace stayed a minute longer. The span carried a steady pulse under fog.  \n[L51] Tiffani leaned against the rail beside him. “You’re bleeding through the wrap.”  \n[L52] A small patch of red showed. “Proof I’m alive.”  \n[L53] She leaned her head against the same rail. “Alive’s overrated.” Her shoulder brushed his. His tremor eased.  \n[L54] They left the Lume burning low on the post, light catching the still‑wet line like a thin scar that refused to brighten.  \n[L55] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L56] \n[L57] ## **Chapter 10: Mobel at the Table (Interlude)**\n# 【6†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file6\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Costs?”  \n[L2] “Palm burn, two Shade skins, no ring.”  \n[L3] He nodded. “Fray band notch from clamp.”  \n[L4] “Clinic if it splits,” she said.  \n[L5] He flexed his hand, skin tight but whole. “Policy met.”  \n[L6] The fog thinned enough to show the river moving below, dark and quick. A bell buoy swung under the span, knocking once, then going quiet.  \n[L7] Mel Quarr arrived before they could finish stowing gear. Her coat carried old rain. “Strathmoor. What did it try this time?”  \n[L8] “Thread drifted north,” Tiffani said. “Quarter and a hair. Reset, Slaked, Veiled. No hold.”  \n[L9] Mel bent to the coupler, touched the new seam, and closed her eyes. The city’s best witness never looked; she felt. “You gave it a longer walk,” she said. “Good. The old route was too proud.”  \n[L10] “Witness?” Tiffani asked.  \n[L11] “Witness,” Mel said, straightening. She wiped her fingers on her coat and signed Brighlee’s ledger. “Post it.”  \n[L12] Brighlee clipped the cleared slip under the droplet. The ink still wet, she waited for the air to fix it. “Law in effect.”  \n[L13] The carts began to move again, a single wheel squealing, then catching rhythm. Jhace watched the strain lines across the span ease back into place—steel carrying itself again, the bridge alive but content.  \n[L14] Tiffani leaned on the railing, eyes on the water. “You ever think it remembers the first one who traced it?”  \n[L15] “Bridges don’t remember people. They remember treatment.”  \n[L16] She gave the smallest shrug. “That’s people.”  \n[L17] He didn’t argue. The fog folded them both into the same shape for a while, and that was enough.  \n[L18] They gathered tools, slung packs, and crossed back toward the Underface stairs. Brighlee stayed behind to finish her ink. She always waited for it to dry before moving, superstition or craft—same thing down here.  \n[L19] At the far end, Jhace looked back once. The Veil skins had cooled, light catching on them like dull glass. The seam between plates looked born that way, not repaired. That was the goal: leave no sign of having been needed.  \n[L20] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L21] \n[L22] ## **Chapter 3: Counterbend at Lowcourt Arch**\n[L23] \n[L24] Bell Six came sharp and bright through the Upper shafts, ringing against every pipe that held the morning fog. Lowcourt’s Arch never liked daylight—it showed what the night had hidden. A hairline split ran between the arch stones where the third rib met the walkway bracket, a clean crack widening by breath.  \n[L25] Jhace was halfway up the scaffold before Brighlee finished reading the notice. “Work order eight-eight-one. Counterbend, Lowcourt Arch. Setter Jhace brast, Glass Tiffani veld. Local hold on half-lane until witness clear.” The last word echoed in the hollow under the span.  \n[L26] Tiffani was already on the inner run, hand to the seam. “It’s new,” she said. “Two fingers deep. Warm on one edge.”  \n[L27] Jhace crouched, running his glove along the joint. The air pushed out, steady pulse. “Thermal vein undercut the rib. We bend it back or lose the bracket by Bell Nine.”  \n[L28] She tapped her chalk against the stone, marking the stress lines.\n# 【7†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file7\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Blood had dried brown along one edge. “Grip’s back. Knee’s gone.”  \n[L2] “Clinic.”  \n[L3] “Policy.”  \n[L4] They left the sump cleaner than they found it and walked back into the Underface where steam threw long, soft shadows. By Bell Three, the rumor had reached the Crown. Runners began pulling L/P–43 from Conclave shelves without waiting for orders.  \n[L5] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L6] \n[L7] ## **Chapter 12: Skew Line at Wains Cut**\n[L8] \n[L9] Bell Seven carried dust. The Cut at Wains threw noise in strange directions, the kind that hid faults until they were underfoot. Tiffani and Jhace made the east stair in twenty-seven breaths, short and counted, then stopped at a joint where the floor plates met the wall collar. The line ran straight for two spans and then drifted a hair toward the drain.  \n[L10] “Skew,” Jhace said. “Someone chased a leak with dry trace.”  \n[L11] Tiffani crouched, Lume low. The line was pretty and wrong—neat, thin, and shiny. “Whitequench’s cousin,” she said. “Looks good. Lies.”  \n[L12] Brighlee arrived with the ledger already open. “Work order one-two-one-eight. Line correction, Wains Cut. Local hold on foot.” Functional again; no garnish.  \n[L13] A supervisor from the Cut eased close, hands empty, eyes hungry. “We need it open,” he said. “Our crew kept it neat.”  \n[L14] “Neat isn’t safe,” Tiffani said. “Back two meters.”  \n[L15] He didn’t move. Jhace did, a half-step that put his shoulder between Tiffani and the man. The supervisor took the hint and faded.  \n[L16] “Angle?” Jhace asked.  \n[L17] “Two degrees toward drain for three spans,” Tiffani said, tapping chalk marks. “Then straight to the corner. We pull the lie and give it a path.”  \n[L18] He set two wedges lightly to keep the plate from talking while they corrected the line. “Half-beat Slake,” he said, more to himself than to her.  \n[L19] She cut the wet into the white. The false line lifted like shed skin when the real pressure touched it. It came away in narrow curls that stank faint of solvent. “Shop trace,” she said. “They painted it.”  \n[L20] Jhace swore. “Who?”  \n[L21] “Same lot mark from the Veil recall,” Brighlee said without looking up. “Lonce & Parr sells more than skins.”  \n[L22] Tiffani nodded once, tight. “We post it later. Fix it now.”  \n[L23] She traced the true line with a bead heavy enough to sink into the plate’s shallow grain. Jhace fed from his bladder, steady. They Slaked on the half-beat, then bridged the turn with a small counterline to keep the path honest.  \n[L24] “Pulse?” he asked.  \n[L25] She listened, mouth open. “Level.”  \n[L26] “Veil?”  \n[L27] She warmed a Shade 1 from a Crown lot that had passed her knife test twice this morning and pressed it down. Tap, tap. The seal went dull.  \n[L28] The supervisor edged closer again. “So we’re clear?”  \n[L29] “No,” Tiffani said, not looking up. “We’re safe. Clear when the witness signs.”  \n[L30] Mel Quarr came on the last word as if conjured by impatience. She crouched, laid her palm to the line, and went still. Her nose wrinkled.\n# 【8†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file8\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] A group of apprentices watched from the steps, carrying fresh boards. They whispered to each other, eyes on the burn box. “Double brace rule?” one asked.  \n[L2] Corlexi didn’t look up. “Double brace rule. Within ten rounds.”  \n[L3] The fire spat once. Wax smell thickened. The second placard carried a name he knew—Maren hilt, a good setter who’d cracked a knee when a brace failed two months ago. He held it longer before letting it fall.  \n[L4] On the far side of the square, the Couriers’ Bell office was already laying out the new boards. Ink crews worked off a folding table, stamping wedge glyphs two‑by‑two, copper heads hot enough to hiss when they touched wood. The pattern had changed overnight: old inward braces replaced by paired diagonals, more metal, more safety.  \n[L5] “Quicker this way?” an apprentice asked.  \n[L6] “No,” Corlexi said. “Slower, safer.”  \n[L7] He fed the third placard into the bin. The crowd near the food stalls had gone quiet, watching the smoke. It wasn’t ceremony; it was relief disguised as boredom.  \n[L8] A Conclave clerk crossed the square with a basket of new seals. “Posting priority corners first,” he said, voice careful. “The Conclave wants public compliance before dusk.”  \n[L9] Corlexi wiped sweat from his cheek with the back of his arm. “They always do.”  \n[L10] The clerk hesitated, holding a placard halfway. “You were there for Lowcourt, right?”  \n[L11] “Doesn’t matter where I was.” He took the placard and set it into the fire. “Now I’m here.”  \n[L12] The last of the old signs turned to smoke. He raked the ashes with a long iron rod until the glyphs broke apart. Behind him, the apprentices lifted the new boards, carried them toward the market posts, the stairwells, the corners by the canal. Every new mark would show two wedges instead of one.  \n[L13] A teamster paused his cart to watch. “Another rule already?”  \n[L14] Corlexi turned, voice even. “One that keeps your wheels on the ground.”  \n[L15] The man tipped his cap and moved on.  \n[L16] Corlexi knelt by the ashes, scooped a handful into a small cloth bag, tied it tight. He labeled the bag in careful script: Witness residue — old policy burned clean.  \n[L17] By the time the Bell rang half, the square had changed color. The old blue of single braces had gone gray in the soot, replaced by fresh copper and paint that hadn’t yet dulled. Children traced the new glyphs with their fingers as they passed.  \n[L18] From the east alley came a gust of steam—pipe vent clearing from Alderline’s repair. Corlexi looked up through it, saw the faint shadow of the Conclave tower against the fog. The world was always patching itself faster than people could keep up.  \n[L19] An apprentice returned from the posting run, breath quick. “Every corner’s marked. Last one’s drying by the pump road.”  \n[L20] Corlexi nodded. “Good. When it rains, the wax will tell us which ones hold.”  \n[L21] “What if one doesn’t?”  \n[L22] “Then we bury someone,” he said. “And trace it again.”  \n[L23] The clerk from the Conclave sealed the record page with the last of his ink. “Policy change filed: all Cut locks to carry double brace before Bell Twenty‑two. Witness Corlexi, Market Station.”  \n[L24] He signed his name small, a single curl at the end. “Filed,” he said.  \n[L25] The clerk hesitated.\n# 【9†Standards in Motion.md†file_0000000077ac61f5865c2e2818920c23】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Standards in Motion.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file9\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] On the posted card, the wedge glyph sat clear as a coin. Beneath it, in that same honey dim, the pane’s edge showed safe matte while the brief mist thinned and was gone.\n[L2] \n[L3] # **Chapter 3: Pressline Cadence**\n[L4] \n[L5] Jhace reached the Narrowvale choke ahead of the bell change and found the weather already doing the work of a poor foreman. Rain hit in sheets that turned cobbles to polished iron. Kettle steam bent under the gusts and laid a low fog at shin height. The lane held like a frayed rope in a hand that hadn’t decided to let go or pull. Bell Two’s window was about to end; Bell Four would raise its face. That seam was where lanes came apart.\n[L6] \n[L7] He slung the short bar across his shoulder. It was nothing more than a length of tempered rod with a coin‑thick cap for the taps. The pressline lives in small, clear sounds. He kept the placard kit under his cloak to keep the ridger teeth dry. Brighlee stood already at the caller post, Blue square still showing, fingers at the tie knot, eyes on him. Kylar held a coil of cord and the little hammer like a young man who had understood that hands idle at a choke were a liability.\n[L8] \n[L9] Vendors shoved for last sales before a window shift. A man with red sleeves decided the center lane was his private invention and angled a cart into foot traffic. A girl carrying oil tins bumped a shoulder and swallowed her pain so it wouldn’t leak. Authority noise gathered on the edges like a storm building its own weather. The bell rope twitched down the corridor where it hung from its housing.\n[L10] \n[L11] Jhace set his palm on the caller post to feel the lane’s tone through the wood, the way a bridgewright learns to listen to his spans. The energy had turned granular; there was no flow, only a crowd of particles insulting each other. He took a breath that hurt a little from the morning’s chill and let it out slow. Then he lifted the bar.\n[L12] \n[L13] Two short taps.\n[L14] \n[L15] They landed clean, coin on rod, a sound with borders. The first found the crowd’s ears. The second told them the first wasn’t a mistake. He waited a breath and delivered them again. Two short taps. He kept the spacing with his ribs, not his head.\n[L16] \n[L17] Brighlee flipped the caller square from Blue to Yellow with a quick twist and tied it so it could be cut fast if weather asked. Kylar drifted along the left boundary, pointing the front axle of Red Sleeves back to the heavy lane with a patient hand and the plain authority of someone who belonged to the board.\n[L18] \n[L19] A vendor near the steam carts shoved the man in front of him to prove a point to a third party. The shove shook the middle lane like a wire. Jhace did not look there. He looked at the heads down the way and counted four, then six, that had started to move in sympathy with the taps. Two short. Two short. The cadence did not tell them what to do; it taught them when to do what the board already asked.\n[L20] \n[L21] He used his left hand for the bar, the one that took its orders more reluctantly, to preserve the right for fine work if he needed it. The weather’s needles pushed into the cuff of his sleeve. The rod’s cap rang true; the sound bounced once off the stone face and returned corrected. He saw the first knot slacken where runners had tangled with a barrow and tried to share space.\n# 【10†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file10\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The carts shifted. Law to motion again.  \n[L2] Jhace crouched once more, checking the seam. Wet line dull and even. No bubbles under the veil, wedges seated clean. The air above the trace no longer shimmered. “Blackgate’s quiet.”  \n[L3] Tiffani smiled without teeth, the kind of smile that left the ledger unbalanced. “It remembers when we make it look good.”  \n[L4] They packed in silence. Tools wiped, copper cooled, wedges tapped free. Brighlee closed her ledger with a single snap. The sound meant done.  \n[L5] The first cart eased forward, front wheel passing the seam. The rim rolled the shallow bow and stayed silent. The driver glanced down, then up, gave the small nod that meant you did the thing; I saw you.  \n[L6] They stayed until the third cart cleared, then unhooked from the home ring. Tiffani closed her case. Brighlee walked off, already writing the next line of work that would prove they’d lived this day correctly. Jhace lingered, eyes on the wet mark as it cooled. The steam had gone to thin breath, rising straight and vanishing against the stone.  \n[L7] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L8] \n[L9] ## **Chapter 2: Thread Draw at Strathmoor Span**\n[L10] \n[L11] Bell Four came late, smothered by fog off the river. The air sat wet enough to drink. Jhace crossed the east truss walkway, his boots scraping the rust film that always rose before thaw. Strathmoor Span was a good bridge when it wanted to be—broad face, six weight lines—but it carried a habit of slow leaks under the mid-beam couplers. Every thaw, someone found the problem too late.  \n[L12] Tiffani was already halfway down the central run, kneeling over a length of open face, one palm on the metal, one arm buried to the elbow in a coil pit. She didn’t look up. “Thread slipped. South coupler. Field’s off-center. You feel it?”  \n[L13] He knelt beside her and laid a hand flat on the deck. The vibration came up through his glove—a low push that staggered every third pulse. Not a crack, just uneven tension. “Field pulling north. Thread’s walked a quarter.”  \n[L14] “Quarter?”  \n[L15] “Quarter and a hair.”  \n[L16] She made a mark on the plate with chalk, short horizontal dash. “That’s enough to make a vein forget itself.”  \n[L17] Brighlee clanged down the stair, ledger box bumping her hip. “Work order seven-five-six. Thread Draw, Strathmoor. Setter Jhace brast, Glass Tiffani veld, no hold. Bell Four.” She Tempermarked the form, clipped it to the post, and stood aside to let them breathe.  \n[L18] “Keep the carts back two lengths,” Tiffani said. “And watch for echo if the draw pulls too fast.”  \n[L19] Jhace unpacked from his belt: two threaded rods, one brace clamp, half bladder of wet. Nothing else. He slipped the rods through the anchor flanges and tightened both heads finger-firm. “Ready on brace.”  \n[L20] “Home ring?” she asked.  \n[L21] He set his hand to the anchor post. “One hand on home.”  \n[L22] “Half-beat Slake,” she said, and took position above him with the Lume angled down. The fog caught the light and turned the air to milk.  \n[L23] He drew the first line of wet from the north coupler down into the seam. The water hissed where metal met stress.\n# 【11†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file11\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Behind him, the apprentices lifted the new boards, carried them toward the market posts, the stairwells, the corners by the canal. Every new mark would show two wedges instead of one.  \n[L2] A teamster paused his cart to watch. “Another rule already?”  \n[L3] Corlexi turned, voice even. “One that keeps your wheels on the ground.”  \n[L4] The man tipped his cap and moved on.  \n[L5] Corlexi knelt by the ashes, scooped a handful into a small cloth bag, tied it tight. He labeled the bag in careful script: Witness residue — old policy burned clean.  \n[L6] By the time the Bell rang half, the square had changed color. The old blue of single braces had gone gray in the soot, replaced by fresh copper and paint that hadn’t yet dulled. Children traced the new glyphs with their fingers as they passed.  \n[L7] From the east alley came a gust of steam—pipe vent clearing from Alderline’s repair. Corlexi looked up through it, saw the faint shadow of the Conclave tower against the fog. The world was always patching itself faster than people could keep up.  \n[L8] An apprentice returned from the posting run, breath quick. “Every corner’s marked. Last one’s drying by the pump road.”  \n[L9] Corlexi nodded. “Good. When it rains, the wax will tell us which ones hold.”  \n[L10] “What if one doesn’t?”  \n[L11] “Then we bury someone,” he said. “And trace it again.”  \n[L12] The clerk from the Conclave sealed the record page with the last of his ink. “Policy change filed: all Cut locks to carry double brace before Bell Twenty‑two. Witness Corlexi, Market Station.”  \n[L13] He signed his name small, a single curl at the end. “Filed,” he said.  \n[L14] The clerk hesitated. “You burn them clean.”  \n[L15] “Everything burns clean if you give it enough air.”  \n[L16] The clerk left with the ledger. The apprentices lingered, unsure if they were dismissed. Corlexi pointed at the fresh boards stacked by the rail. “Post two more at the bridge ends before Bell Thirteen. People forget fast.”  \n[L17] They moved, boots thudding.  \n[L18] When the square cleared, Corlexi sat on the edge of the ash bin. The fire had gone to coals, orange and low. He held one unburned corner of an old placard, tracing the single‑wedge mark with his thumb. It wasn’t wrong when they made it; it was just small. The city had outgrown small.  \n[L19] He tossed it into the coals and waited for the wax to hiss.  \n[L20] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L21] \n[L22] ## **Chapter 7: Double Fault at Rindle Yard**\n[L23] \n[L24] The Thread was already broken when they got there, hanging like split rope from the upper seam. Bell Two had barely cleared; the air smelled of burnt iron and shame.  \n[L25] Jhace dropped to one knee beside the fault. The brace plates were wrong—the old single set, bent inward, paint flaked. Someone had patched over the crack with tar and hope.  \n[L26] “Double rule went out last Bell,” Tiffani said, standing over him. “This is still single.”  \n[L27] “Work Order’s dated yesterday.” He peeled a tag from the post. The Tempermark was smudged. “Filed before the change.”  \n[L28] “Doesn’t make it safe.”  \n[L29] He ran a finger along the line. “Who set it?”  \n[L30] “Rindle yard foreman signed it. Kesh Hurn.”  \n[L31] “Figures.” He tapped the seam; dull echo.\n# 【12†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file12\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] MIRECOURT Tiffani found only fog. The leak they’d fixed stayed fixed. She wrote steady and turned.  \n[L2] GREYMANTLE CUT Jhace waited at the last step and listened. The lock below moved water in a slow, obedient draw. He didn’t go down. He didn’t need to.  \n[L3] The round took three Bells. They met again where they’d started, at Brighlee’s board. The paper stack had become a neat column with times and marks.  \n[L4] “Count?” Jhace asked.  \n[L5] “Thirteen corners,” Brighlee said. “No failures.” Her fingers were stained gray from page edges. “One junior wrote himself up.”  \n[L6] Tiffani tilted her head toward Gray Arch. “Good.”  \n[L7] Mel Quarr stepped out of the press of bodies, hair stuck to her face, coat open. “I read your marks as I walked,” she said. “This is what it looks like when a city learns.”  \n[L8] Jhace stretched his burned arm with slow care. “Or when it chooses not to forget.”  \n[L9] Mel’s mouth twitched. “Don’t get poetic.”  \n[L10] He didn’t.  \n[L11] She signed the top sheet without kneeling this time. “I’ll witness the round as a set. Post the list.”  \n[L12] Tiffani wiped her knife. “Costs?”  \n[L13] Jhace looked at his hand. The split had closed, but the skin was thick and new. “Grip returning. Knee still wrong.”  \n[L14] “Dry mouth,” Tiffani said. “Low sugar.”  \n[L15] “Clinic,” Mel said. “Both of you. Salee’s posted new slips.”  \n[L16] They didn’t argue.  \n[L17] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L18] \n[L19] ## **Chapter 14: Salee’s Slip (Interlude)**\n[L20] \n[L21] Bell Three at the Clinic rang like the edge of a cool bowl. Salee stood behind the counter with a stack of blue slips and a stamp that smelled faintly of lemon oil. Workers came in pairs or alone, shoulders straight until they saw the color and then they sagged.  \n[L22] “Name,” she said to the first.  \n[L23] “Jhace brast,” Brighlee answered for him, pushing him forward by the elbow, not looking at his face like dignity required.  \n[L24] Salee didn’t blink. “Burn, persistent. Knee strain.” She wrote fast, neat. “Rest prescription: one Bell immediate, two after shift. Paid.”  \n[L25] Jhace started to object. Salee slid the slip into his hand without touching his skin. “Policy,” she said. “Posted.”  \n[L26] He looked at the wall where the placard hung beside the clinic door. The words were plain: Mandatory rest after consecutive shifts exceeding four Bells. Paid. Enforcement: Clinic stamp overrides work order.  \n[L27] Tiffani stood to the side, color back in her face but eyes ringed. Salee wrote her a second slip: Dehydration, Shade error corrected. Rest: one Bell. Paid.  \n[L28] A foreman edged up, hat in hands. “We’re light on crew.”  \n[L29] Salee stamped a third slip and handed it to him. “Then reschedule. That’s the point.”  \n[L30] He opened his mouth. Closed it. Took the paper.  \n[L31] Mel Quarr signed the margin of the new placard with a small, precise hand. “Witness,” she said, not loud, but no one missed it.  \n[L32] Corlexi passed through with a satchel of old clinic forms headed for the burn barrel. He touched the edge of the new sign like it might be hot.\n# 【13†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file13\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Bell Three at the Clinic rang like the edge of a cool bowl. Salee stood behind the counter with a stack of blue slips and a stamp that smelled faintly of lemon oil. Workers came in pairs or alone, shoulders straight until they saw the color and then they sagged.  \n[L2] “Name,” she said to the first.  \n[L3] “Jhace brast,” Brighlee answered for him, pushing him forward by the elbow, not looking at his face like dignity required.  \n[L4] Salee didn’t blink. “Burn, persistent. Knee strain.” She wrote fast, neat. “Rest prescription: one Bell immediate, two after shift. Paid.”  \n[L5] Jhace started to object. Salee slid the slip into his hand without touching his skin. “Policy,” she said. “Posted.”  \n[L6] He looked at the wall where the placard hung beside the clinic door. The words were plain: Mandatory rest after consecutive shifts exceeding four Bells. Paid. Enforcement: Clinic stamp overrides work order.  \n[L7] Tiffani stood to the side, color back in her face but eyes ringed. Salee wrote her a second slip: Dehydration, Shade error corrected. Rest: one Bell. Paid.  \n[L8] A foreman edged up, hat in hands. “We’re light on crew.”  \n[L9] Salee stamped a third slip and handed it to him. “Then reschedule. That’s the point.”  \n[L10] He opened his mouth. Closed it. Took the paper.  \n[L11] Mel Quarr signed the margin of the new placard with a small, precise hand. “Witness,” she said, not loud, but no one missed it.  \n[L12] Corlexi passed through with a satchel of old clinic forms headed for the burn barrel. He touched the edge of the new sign like it might be hot. “About time,” he said.  \n[L13] Mobel from the Mercers’ Table arrived with a tin of boiled sweets and set them on the counter. “For compliance,” he said. “Not as bribe.”  \n[L14] Salee cracked a smile with one side of her mouth. “Accepted.”  \n[L15] She stamped without pause for an hour. The counter filled with the small sounds of paper, the big silence of people not arguing. At the half-Bell, she replaced the ink pad with a fresh one and posted the second part of the policy beneath the first: Repeat offenders: mandatory two-Bell rest. Dock illegal.  \n[L16] A boy from the Underface brought his mother’s note: Breath short in cold. Salee stamped that too, then handed the boy a sweet and a folded copy of the rest placard. “Post this at her workroom,” she said. “High where she can see it.”  \n[L17] He nodded, small and serious.  \n[L18] When the Bell chimed the quarter, Tiffani and Jhace sat side by side on a bench under the window. They didn’t speak. Their shoulders dropped at the same moment like a weight setting. Salee watched without looking like she was watching and decided not to put that on any form.  \n[L19] She finished the stack, wiped the stamp clean, and posted the day’s count on a small slate: Rest slips issued: 38\\. Refusals: 0\\.  \n[L20] Outside the clinic, the city sounded exactly the same as before. Inside, it didn’t.  \n[L21] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L22] \n[L23] ## **Chapter 15: Small Fix at Lantern Stairs**\n[L24] \n[L25] Bell Six came soft along Lantern Stairs, filtered through laundry lines and morning cook smoke. The call was a polite one: minor creak at the mid-landing brace. No one rushed. They went anyway.\n# 【14†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file14\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Thermal vein tore through the splice. You feel the air?”  \n[L2] He crouched, palm to conduit. The heat bit through the glove fast. “Alive and angry. Vein’s running blind.”  \n[L3] “Blind means fast,” she said. “And if it finds the main, we’ll have a blow under the market.”  \n[L4] “Field direction?”  \n[L5] “East to west, downhill. No counter. Just run.”  \n[L6] Brighlee appeared, collar up against the heat. “Work order one-one-five-four. Heat Splice under Alderline. Setter Jhace brast, Glass Tiffani veld. Local hold to foot traffic until clear.” She Tempermarked the droplet board with shaking fingers; the wax nearly ran before it set.  \n[L7] “Chain it,” Tiffani said.  \n[L8] Jhace fastened his tether to the wall ring, one hand gripping it. “One hand on home.” The metal burned even through his sleeve. “We wedge the run first.”  \n[L9] “Use the short wedges,” she said. “The long ones will swell.”  \n[L10] He pulled two half-lengths from his pouch, jammed the first at the upper seam of the duct mouth, the second two spans below. He hammered them in until the pitch rose high and sharp.  \n[L11] The duct noise shifted—deep to shallow, like breath caught in the chest.  \n[L12] “Trace wet,” she said.  \n[L13] He thumbed the bladder cap, drew water into the copper nib, and began the run. The first line hissed as it met the heat; vapor curled into ribbons. He kept the nib steady, shoulder over hand, driving water into the split vein until the color under the metal dulled from white to gray.  \n[L14] Tiffani moved her Lume rod closer. “Shade four to six,” she said. “Keep the bead thick.”  \n[L15] He adjusted his wrist, laying the second line parallel, two fingers apart. Heat surged under his hand—vein testing him—but the wet held. Steam rolled off the metal in heavy breaths.  \n[L16] “Half-beat Slake,” she said.  \n[L17] He let the wet rest, pressure through shoulder, breath slow. The line steadied. The vein’s pulse dropped.  \n[L18] “Still wants a mouth,” she said.  \n[L19] “I’ll give it one.”  \n[L20] He ran a short feeder line from the end of his first trace to a small vent slit above the duct. The air drew through it, hissing once, then falling quiet.  \n[L21] The heat at his glove softened; the metal under his palm stopped twitching. Tiffani’s hand brushed his wrist once, not comfort but calibration, steadying his wrist until his pulse matched the duct.  \n[L22] “Slake complete,” she said. “Now Veil.”  \n[L23] He stepped aside. She drew a Shade 2 skin from her case, warmed it between her hands, and pressed it over the splice. Steam rose and hung there. Her fingers moved quick—tap tap tap in sets of seven. The seam went dark, settled.  \n[L24] Brighlee’s pen scratched. “Heat Splice contained, Veil applied, local hold remains until witness.”  \n[L25] “Tell them we’ll have it cleared by Bell Nine,” Tiffani said.  \n[L26] Brighlee nodded and climbed back up the tunnel steps to keep the onlookers from drifting too close.  \n[L27] Jhace checked his gloves. The leather had gone glossy, almost translucent. “Burn through,” he muttered.  \n[L28] “Palm?” she asked.  \n[L29] “Blister later. Not yet.”  \n[L30] “Clinic if it lifts,” she said, without looking away from the seam.  \n[L31] He grunted.  \n[L32] The air shifted again—cooler this time.\n# 【15†Standards in Motion.md†file_000000003234622f8cb6d81827b4b5f1】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Standards in Motion.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file15\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] On the posted card, the wedge glyph sat clear as a coin. Beneath it, in that same honey dim, the pane’s edge showed safe matte while the brief mist thinned and was gone.\n[L2] \n[L3] # **Chapter 3: Pressline Cadence**\n[L4] \n[L5] Jhace reached the Narrowvale choke ahead of the bell change and found the weather already doing the work of a poor foreman. Rain hit in sheets that turned cobbles to polished iron. Kettle steam bent under the gusts and laid a low fog at shin height. The lane held like a frayed rope in a hand that hadn’t decided to let go or pull. Bell Two’s window was about to end; Bell Four would raise its face. That seam was where lanes came apart.\n[L6] \n[L7] He slung the short bar across his shoulder. It was nothing more than a length of tempered rod with a coin‑thick cap for the taps. The pressline lives in small, clear sounds. He kept the placard kit under his cloak to keep the ridger teeth dry. Brighlee stood already at the caller post, Blue square still showing, fingers at the tie knot, eyes on him. Kylar held a coil of cord and the little hammer like a young man who had understood that hands idle at a choke were a liability.\n[L8] \n[L9] Vendors shoved for last sales before a window shift. A man with red sleeves decided the center lane was his private invention and angled a cart into foot traffic. A girl carrying oil tins bumped a shoulder and swallowed her pain so it wouldn’t leak. Authority noise gathered on the edges like a storm building its own weather. The bell rope twitched down the corridor where it hung from its housing.\n[L10] \n[L11] Jhace set his palm on the caller post to feel the lane’s tone through the wood, the way a bridgewright learns to listen to his spans. The energy had turned granular; there was no flow, only a crowd of particles insulting each other. He took a breath that hurt a little from the morning’s chill and let it out slow. Then he lifted the bar.\n[L12] \n[L13] Two short taps.\n[L14] \n[L15] They landed clean, coin on rod, a sound with borders. The first found the crowd’s ears. The second told them the first wasn’t a mistake. He waited a breath and delivered them again. Two short taps. He kept the spacing with his ribs, not his head.\n[L16] \n[L17] Brighlee flipped the caller square from Blue to Yellow with a quick twist and tied it so it could be cut fast if weather asked. Kylar drifted along the left boundary, pointing the front axle of Red Sleeves back to the heavy lane with a patient hand and the plain authority of someone who belonged to the board.\n[L18] \n[L19] A vendor near the steam carts shoved the man in front of him to prove a point to a third party. The shove shook the middle lane like a wire. Jhace did not look there. He looked at the heads down the way and counted four, then six, that had started to move in sympathy with the taps. Two short. Two short. The cadence did not tell them what to do; it taught them when to do what the board already asked.\n[L20] \n[L21] He used his left hand for the bar, the one that took its orders more reluctantly, to preserve the right for fine work if he needed it. The weather’s needles pushed into the cuff of his sleeve. The rod’s cap rang true; the sound bounced once off the stone face and returned corrected. He saw the first knot slacken where runners had tangled with a barrow and tried to share space.\n# 【16†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file16\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The air changed tone—low, whole.  \n[L2] Mel Quarr appeared at the top of the rise, coat soaked, hair stuck to her face. “Talk.”  \n[L3] “Joined feed,” Jhace said. “Trace holding.”  \n[L4] She crouched by the seam. “Both lines live?”  \n[L5] “Equalized.”  \n[L6] She pressed her ear to the plate. Long pause. “Steady,” she said. “For now.”  \n[L7] “For now gets carts moving.”  \n[L8] “Not until I sign.” She pulled her pen.  \n[L9] The ground rumbled again, small but rising. Everyone froze. The seam under her hand pulsed once, hard.  \n[L10] “That wasn’t feed,” Mel said. “Expansion.”  \n[L11] “Backflow,” Tiffani said. “Pressure from the Crown line.”  \n[L12] Jhace grabbed the release wrench. “We vent.”  \n[L13] “You open that without brace, you’ll shear the plate,” Mel barked.  \n[L14] “Then we brace.” He set another wedge, deep, metal ringing like struck Bells.  \n[L15] Tiffani cut light to zero, then reopened to full. “Still rising.”  \n[L16] “Three turns,” Jhace said, wrench on the valve.  \n[L17] “Half‑turn only.”  \n[L18] “Three.” He spun once, twice. The seam screamed, then sighed—long and hollow. Steam shot up, scalding.  \n[L19] Then the sound shifted: deep, low exhale of weight finding balance.  \n[L20] Mel pressed her palm to the plate. “Pressure’s gone. Breathing normal.”  \n[L21] “We clear?” he asked.  \n[L22] “HALT lifts at Bell Nine.”  \n[L23] Brighlee’s voice cut across the rise: “Witnessed.”  \n[L24] Tiffani let out a sound close to a laugh. “You’re an idiot.”  \n[L25] “Functional idiot.”  \n[L26] “Still counts.” She rolled his sleeve. Skin blistered pink. “Clinic, now.”  \n[L27] He nodded.  \n[L28] Mel wrote the clearance with hands that weren’t steady yet. “Policy note: overbind feed must split before marriage.”  \n[L29] Brighlee wrote. “Logged.”  \n[L30] Carts began to move again, careful. The crowd thinned with the kind of silence near‑misses earn.  \n[L31] Jhace watched the line. “We nearly lost it.”  \n[L32] Tiffani sat beside him, shoulder to shoulder. “We didn’t.” Her hand found his burned wrist. His breathing evened.  \n[L33] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L34] \n[L35] ## **Chapter 9: Slow Bell at Mirecourt**\n[L36] \n[L37] Bell Ten dragged. Fog held low. Jhace hadn’t slept since Narrowvale. His burned arm still stung under the wrap.  \n[L38] The summons said minor leak, Mirecourt span. Nothing in the city was ever minor.  \n[L39] They reached the gate face to find condensation drip from the inner rail, steady. Not a break. Worse was the sound—a hollow tick between the Bells like a wound undecided.  \n[L40] Tiffani crouched. “Overbind residue. Narrowvale runoff hit the lower feeds. Pressure’s wrong.”  \n[L41] He set his pack down slow. His hand didn’t want to close around the latch. “We shouldn’t be working.”  \n[L42] “You say that every time you’re bleeding.”  \n[L43] “Not every time.”  \n[L44] “Name one you didn’t.”  \n[L45] He didn’t answer.  \n[L46] Brighlee came up the incline, voice rasped. “Work order four‑eight‑one. Leak containment, Mirecourt. Bell Ten to Eleven.” Functional. She posted and stepped back.  \n[L47] “Handle trace,” Tiffani said.\n# 【17†The Line That Holds.md†file-X2o7j2QCrpYXDdCJvEa1rH】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Line That Holds.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file17\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Tiffani checked feedback. “Trace reads steady. If it fails, it’ll do it politely.”  \n[L2] “Polite’s new.”  \n[L3] “Maybe the city’s learning.”  \n[L4] He sat back, wiping sweat with his good sleeve. “You’re shaking.”  \n[L5] “So are you.”  \n[L6] “Then we match.”  \n[L7] “Don’t make poetry out of exhaustion.”  \n[L8] He didn’t.  \n[L9] Mel Quarr appeared at the top of the ramp, as tired as the rest. “Report.”  \n[L10] “Minor leak, sealed,” Tiffani said. “No hold. Feed equalized.”  \n[L11] Mel knelt, pressed her hand to the plate. “Holding.” She looked at Jhace’s arm. “You working injured again?”  \n[L12] “Clinic after shift.”  \n[L13] “Clinic now. I’ll send a slip.”  \n[L14] Brighlee pinned the form to the board. “Work order four‑eight‑one complete. Bell Eleven.”  \n[L15] “Costs?” Mel asked.  \n[L16] “Burn reopening,” Jhace said.  \n[L17] “Dehydration,” Tiffani added.  \n[L18] Mel wrote both. “Clinic, mandatory.”  \n[L19] They didn’t argue.  \n[L20] When the others left, Jhace stayed a minute longer. The span carried a steady pulse under fog.  \n[L21] Tiffani leaned against the rail beside him. “You’re bleeding through the wrap.”  \n[L22] A small patch of red showed. “Proof I’m alive.”  \n[L23] She leaned her head against the same rail. “Alive’s overrated.” Her shoulder brushed his. His tremor eased.  \n[L24] They left the Lume burning low on the post, light catching the still‑wet line like a thin scar that refused to brighten.  \n[L25] \\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\\_\n[L26] \n[L27] ## **Chapter 10: Mobel at the Table (Interlude)**\n[L28] \n[L29] Bell Two chimed through the Mercers’ Quarter, each note sliding through the lattice rafters above the weighing floor. Mobel had been there since dawn, coat off, sleeves rolled, the kind of tired that doesn’t show until it stops moving.  \n[L30] The scales in front of him were old Crown brass, polished to dull gold. They didn’t lie often, but they could be coaxed. The night auditors had caught three weight sets marked light in the middle coin. Not enough to hang a merchant, enough to make the Conclave twitch.  \n[L31] He placed one suspect weight on the pan, thumbed the notch along its rim. “Filed too deep,” he said to his clerk. “Half‑grain shave.”  \n[L32] The clerk squinted. “Still legal?”  \n[L33] Mobel didn’t answer. He flicked the weight off the pan. It hit\nVisible: 0% - 89%",
